content.content:
115	### Quality & Verification
116	
117	**Assay** — an examination of completed work to determine its quality. When an artificer completes a commission, the work is assayed before it's accepted. Does it work? Does it meet the spec? Does it break anything? System mapping: automated testing, CI checks, integration verification. The assayer could be a role — or it could be an engine (mechanical quality checks) with an Oracle consulted for judgment calls.
118	
119	**Hallmark** — a quality stamp applied to work that has passed assay. Hallmarked work is trusted; unhallmarked work is provisional. A workshop might refuse unhallmarked contributions. System mapping: CI passing, code review approval, merge criteria. The hallmark is what gates completed work entering the workshop permanently.
120	
121	**Trial by Craft** — a demonstration of skill required before a member is trusted with certain work. An apprentice artificer might need to complete trial commissions in a sandboxed environment before being dispatched to real workshops. System mapping: agent evaluation/qualification — running test commissions to verify a member's capabilities before giving them production access. This ties into the Academy: the Academy trains, the trial proves.
122	
123	### Lifecycle & Ceremony
124	
125	**Muster** — the act of assembling members for a commission. Before dispatch, the system musters the required members: checks the roster, verifies availability, confirms the sage is ready, ensures the artificer is active. System mapping: the pre-dispatch validation step. Currently implicit in `send`, but formalizing it as "muster" makes it a named, debuggable phase.
126	
127	**Vigil** — a period of watching and waiting. After a commission is dispatched, someone (or something) keeps vigil — monitoring progress, watching for failures, waiting for completion. System mapping: the background monitoring that checks commission status, detects failures, and triggers alerts. Currently a manual status check. An engine could keep vigil automatically.
128	
129	**Rite of Naming** — the ceremony by which a new anima is called into being and given their identity. Not just "add to database" — the naming is when the spirit receives its name and seal, becoming a distinct presence in the guild. The rite could include an initial training session at the Academy (aspirant phase), a trial by craft, and formal induction to the roster as an active anima.
130	
131	**Requiem** — the process of retiring an anima. Not dissolution — the anima's record persists in the register forever, their seals remain on their work, their lore is preserved. But the spirit passes to rest: `retired` state, no longer manifested. System mapping: a formal process that includes archiving the anima's current instructions and recording why they were retired.
132	
133	### Communication & Coordination
134	
135	**Herald** — a specialized engine (or role?) that announces events across the guild. Commission completed. New member inducted. Petition granted. House chartered. The herald doesn't decide anything — it broadcasts. System mapping: an event/notification system. Webhooks, log events, Slack notifications, whatever — the herald is the abstraction over "tell everyone something happened."
136	
137	**Summons** — a formal request for a specific member's attention. Different from a message in the inbox — a summons is urgent and targeted. "The Oracle is summoned to examine the authentication module." System mapping: a high-priority, synchronous invocation of a specific member, as opposed to async inbox delivery. *(Naming conflict resolved — "summon" removed from foundational metaphor, engine renamed to "compose", later to "manifest.")*
138	
139	**Parley** — a structured conversation between two or more members. When the Master Sage convenes the Council, that's a parley. When an artificer encounters something unexpected and needs to consult the Oracle mid-commission, that's a parley. System mapping: agent-to-agent communication within a commission lifecycle. This is the multi-agent coordination primitive — how do two AI agents actually talk to each other?
140	
141	**Dispatch** — already used informally, but worth naming explicitly. A dispatch is the act of sending a member on a commission. It includes the muster (validation), the writ (authorization), and the actual invocation. System mapping: the send command, but decomposed into named phases.
142	
143	### Spatial & Territorial
144	
145	**Ward** — a protective boundary around a workshop or house. Wards define what members can and cannot do within a space. "No force-pushes to main." "No modifications to the auth module without Oracle consultation." System mapping: branch protection rules, path-based permissions, pre-commit hooks, CODEOWNERS — all the guardrails that prevent members from damaging work.
146	
147	**Commons** — external resources the guild draws on but does not build or maintain. GitHub, AWS accounts, credentials for external services, docker sockets, API keys. The guild operates on the commons; it does not own them. Registered in the guildhall as metadata; credentials and secrets live outside the guildhall. Animas are told which commons are available at dispatch.
148	
149	### The Weird Stuff (speculative, possibly dumb, possibly brilliant)
150	
151	**Familiar** — what if, instead of being a synonym for engine, a familiar is an engine *bound to a specific member*? Every member could have a personal familiar — a set of mechanical scripts tailored to that member's role and habits. The artificer's familiar manages their git workflow. The sage's familiar gathers codebase context before consultation. System mapping: per-role automation that wraps the agent invocation with role-specific pre/post processing.
152	
153	**Enchantment** — a persistent augmentation applied to a workshop or tool. An enchanted workshop might have automatic linting, type-checking, or test-running on every commit. An enchanted CLI tool might have built-in telemetry. Enchantments are set-and-forget improvements that make everything in their scope better. System mapping: CI/CD pipelines, git hooks, automated quality tooling — but framed as *enhancements to the artifact* rather than external processes.
154	
155	**Prophecy** — a prediction about future work, made by the Oracle or Master Sage. "If we continue on this architecture, we will hit scaling problems in the auth module within 3 commissions." "The test suite will become the bottleneck before the code does." Prophecies are speculative and may be wrong, but they inform prioritization. System mapping: AI-generated technical forecasting. Feed an Oracle the full codebase and ask "what's going to break next?" Use prophecies to generate petitions proactively.
156	
157	**Ritual** — a prescribed sequence of actions performed by engines and members in coordination. A deployment is a ritual. A release is a ritual. Onboarding a new workshop is a ritual. Rituals are repeatable, documented, and partially mechanical (engine steps) and partially intelligent (member steps). System mapping: runbooks, playbooks, CI/CD pipelines with manual gates — any multi-step process that blends automation with judgment.
158	
159	**Pilgrimage** — a commission undertaken not for deliverables, but for knowledge. Send an artificer (or sage) to explore an unfamiliar codebase, library, or technology — not to build anything, but to return with lore. The lore is then available to the guild for future commissions. System mapping: exploratory spikes, research tasks, "go figure out how this library works and write up what you learn." The output is a tome or lore entry, not code.
160	
161	---
162	
163	## Weak Concepts (kept for reference, minimal utility)
164	
165	These concepts have been considered and found to have limited functional value. They're preserved here in case future needs give them a purpose, but they don't currently earn a place in the metaphor or architecture.
166	
167	**School** — the identity and disposition produced by a curriculum. What animas "come from," what outcomes accumulate against. In theory, querying "how did Thomson-school animas perform?" is a school-level question. In practice, the curriculum name already carries this identity — `training/curricula/thomson/v2.md` is a Thomson artifact without needing "school" as a separate concept. School is a human-legible grouping label, not a system entity. No engine, implement, or Ledger table references "school" as a distinct thing. If the guild ever needs to track performance or reputation across curriculum versions, the concept might earn its place. Until then, it's just another name for "curriculum name."
168

file.content:
### Quality & Verification

**Assay** — an examination of completed work to determine its quality. When an artificer completes a commission, the work is assayed before it's accepted. Does it work? Does it meet the spec? Does it break anything? System mapping: automated testing, CI checks, integration verification. The assayer could be a role — or it could be an engine (mechanical quality checks) with an Oracle consulted for judgment calls.

**Hallmark** — a quality stamp applied to work that has passed assay. Hallmarked work is trusted; unhallmarked work is provisional. A workshop might refuse unhallmarked contributions. System mapping: CI passing, code review approval, merge criteria. The hallmark is what gates completed work entering the workshop permanently.

**Trial by Craft** — a demonstration of skill required before a member is trusted with certain work. An apprentice artificer might need to complete trial commissions in a sandboxed environment before being dispatched to real workshops. System mapping: agent evaluation/qualification — running test commissions to verify a member's capabilities before giving them production access. This ties into the Academy: the Academy trains, the trial proves.

### Lifecycle & Ceremony

**Muster** — the act of assembling members for a commission. Before dispatch, the system musters the required members: checks the roster, verifies availability, confirms the sage is ready, ensures the artificer is active. System mapping: the pre-dispatch validation step. Currently implicit in `send`, but formalizing it as "muster" makes it a named, debuggable phase.

**Vigil** — a period of watching and waiting. After a commission is dispatched, someone (or something) keeps vigil — monitoring progress, watching for failures, waiting for completion. System mapping: the background monitoring that checks commission status, detects failures, and triggers alerts. Currently a manual status check. An engine could keep vigil automatically.

**Rite of Naming** — the ceremony by which a new anima is called into being and given their identity. Not just "add to database" — the naming is when the spirit receives its name and seal, becoming a distinct presence in the guild. The rite could include an initial training session at the Academy (aspirant phase), a trial by craft, and formal induction to the roster as an active anima.

**Requiem** — the process of retiring an anima. Not dissolution — the anima's record persists in the register forever, their seals remain on their work, their lore is preserved. But the spirit passes to rest: `retired` state, no longer manifested. System mapping: a formal process that includes archiving the anima's current instructions and recording why they were retired.

### Communication & Coordination

**Herald** — a specialized engine (or role?) that announces events across the guild. Commission completed. New member inducted. Petition granted. House chartered. The herald doesn't decide anything — it broadcasts. System mapping: an event/notification system. Webhooks, log events, Slack notifications, whatever — the herald is the abstraction over "tell everyone something happened."

**Summons** — a formal request for a specific member's attention. Different from a message in the inbox — a summons is urgent and targeted. "The Oracle is summoned to examine the authentication module." System mapping: a high-priority, synchronous invocation of a specific member, as opposed to async inbox delivery. *(Naming conflict resolved — "summon" removed from foundational metaphor, engine renamed to "compose", later to "manifest.")*

**Parley** — a structured conversation between two or more members. When the Master Sage convenes the Council, that's a parley. When an artificer encounters something unexpected and needs to consult the Oracle mid-commission, that's a parley. System mapping: agent-to-agent communication within a commission lifecycle. This is the multi-agent coordination primitive — how do two AI agents actually talk to each other?

**Dispatch** — already used informally, but worth naming explicitly. A dispatch is the act of sending a member on a commission. It includes the muster (validation), the writ (authorization), and the actual invocation. System mapping: the send command, but decomposed into named phases.

### Spatial & Territorial

**Ward** — a protective boundary around a workshop or house. Wards define what members can and cannot do within a space. "No force-pushes to main." "No modifications to the auth module without Oracle consultation." System mapping: branch protection rules, path-based permissions, pre-commit hooks, CODEOWNERS — all the guardrails that prevent members from damaging work.

**Commons** — external resources the guild draws on but does not build or maintain. GitHub, AWS accounts, credentials for external services, docker sockets, API keys. The guild operates on the commons; it does not own them. Registered in the guildhall as metadata; credentials and secrets live outside the guildhall. Animas are told which commons are available at dispatch.

### The Weird Stuff (speculative, possibly dumb, possibly brilliant)

**Familiar** — what if, instead of being a synonym for engine, a familiar is an engine *bound to a specific member*? Every member could have a personal familiar — a set of mechanical scripts tailored to that member's role and habits. The artificer's familiar manages their git workflow. The sage's familiar gathers codebase context before consultation. System mapping: per-role automation that wraps the agent invocation with role-specific pre/post processing.

**Enchantment** — a persistent augmentation applied to a workshop or tool. An enchanted workshop might have automatic linting, type-checking, or test-running on every commit. An enchanted CLI tool might have built-in telemetry. Enchantments are set-and-forget improvements that make everything in their scope better. System mapping: CI/CD pipelines, git hooks, automated quality tooling — but framed as *enhancements to the artifact* rather than external processes.

**Prophecy** — a prediction about future work, made by the Oracle or Master Sage. "If we continue on this architecture, we will hit scaling problems in the auth module within 3 commissions." "The test suite will become the bottleneck before the code does." Prophecies are speculative and may be wrong, but they inform prioritization. System mapping: AI-generated technical forecasting. Feed an Oracle the full codebase and ask "what's going to break next?" Use prophecies to generate petitions proactively.

**Ritual** — a prescribed sequence of actions performed by engines and members in coordination. A deployment is a ritual. A release is a ritual. Onboarding a new workshop is a ritual. Rituals are repeatable, documented, and partially mechanical (engine steps) and partially intelligent (member steps). System mapping: runbooks, playbooks, CI/CD pipelines with manual gates — any multi-step process that blends automation with judgment.

**Pilgrimage** — a commission undertaken not for deliverables, but for knowledge. Send an artificer (or sage) to explore an unfamiliar codebase, library, or technology — not to build anything, but to return with lore. The lore is then available to the guild for future commissions. System mapping: exploratory spikes, research tasks, "go figure out how this library works and write up what you learn." The output is a tome or lore entry, not code.

---

## Weak Concepts (kept for reference, minimal utility)

These concepts have been considered and found to have limited functional value. They're preserved here in case future needs give them a purpose, but they don't currently earn a place in the metaphor or architecture.

**School** — the identity and disposition produced by a curriculum. What animas "come from," what outcomes accumulate against. In theory, querying "how did Thomson-school animas perform?" is a school-level question. In practice, the curriculum name already carries this identity — `training/curricula/thomson/v2.md` is a Thomson artifact without needing "school" as a separate concept. School is a human-legible grouping label, not a system entity. No engine, implement, or Ledger table references "school" as a distinct thing. If the guild ever needs to track performance or reputation across curriculum versions, the concept might earn its place. Until then, it's just another name for "curriculum name."

content.text:
Good intuition check: of the five, **stuck-rig detection** and **failure/retry policy** are the tightest pair — detection produces a terminal "this attempt is dead" signal; retry policy decides what to do with it. Picking them up together keeps the design honest. And there's a vocabulary dividend: the **Vigil** term already sits in the tome describing this exact shape ("a period of watching and waiting… monitoring progress, watching for failures"), so we'd be lighting up latent metaphor rather than inventing new.

Before diving into forks, let me frame what we're actually defending against.

## What "stuck" can mean for a rig

1. **Engine hung** — process alive, no progress (no tokens, no tool calls).
2. **Engine crashed** — process died without clean exit.
3. **Engine exits clean but leaves writ in a weird state** (partial seal, missing deliverable).
4. **Slow but alive** — legitimate long-running work, or the model is just grinding.
5. **Host/daemon restart** — rig orphaned, engine gone, writ still marked dispatched.
6. **External-dependency wedge** — MCP tool-server 500s, guild-side bug, etc.

In a serial hopper (maxConcurrentEngines: 1), **any** of these stalls the whole pipeline until detected. The cost of "one stuck rig" is "no further work until a human notices." That makes stuck detection genuinely load-bearing for the MVP.

## An MVP shape I'd propose

Keep it mechanically simple — the point is *detection is present*, not *detection is clever*.

- **Signal: progress on the session record.** An engine that's actually working appends to its session record (tokens, tool calls, writ updates). Staleness of the record ≈ staleness of the rig. Cheap to observe, already recorded.
- **Detector: timeout on last-progress, generous threshold.** Something like "no session-record growth for N minutes" — N large enough (say 15–30 min) that real work doesn't trip it. False positive cost = one retry. False negative cost = hopper stalls. Bias toward false positives.
- **Reaper: piggybacks on Spider's poll.** Spider already walks dispatched work each tick. One more check — "for each dispatched rig, is its session record stale?" — adds negligible cost and avoids a new apparatus.
- **Action on detection:** kill the process (if alive), mark rig `failed` with `status.spider.failureCause = 'stalled'`, the handoff to retry policy takes it from there.
- **Orphan cleanup on daemon boot:** on Spider startup, any rig marked `dispatched` whose process is gone is reaped the same way. That handles case #5 for free.

Timeouts + reaper, roughly as the click goal says — but the key move is **using session-record activity as the progress signal** rather than wall-clock alone. That cleanly separates "slow" from "stuck."

## Handing off to retry policy

Once a rig is reaped, retry policy owns the decision. The forks here:

1. **What gets retried?** All stalled rigs? Only first attempt? Depends on failure cause?
   - My default: **yes by default, once, for all non-patron-declined failures**. Bold policies can come later when we have data.
2. **New rig on same writ, or new writ?**
   - Same writ, new rig. The writ is the obligation; rig is the attempt. This is literally the model the multi-rig refactor exists to formalize — but the combined brief→implementation rig already treats the brief as the single writ, so we can start here without waiting for multi-rig to land. A rig counter on the writ (`status.spider.attempts`) is enough.
3. **Retry limit?**
   - **Default N=2 attempts total**, then writ → `stuck` with reason 'retries exhausted'. Human triages from there.
4. **Starvation fairness (the parenthetical in the click goal)?**
   - In a serial hopper with N=1 concurrency, the fairness question degenerates: there *is* no starvation because everything is head-of-line already. The parenthetical was written for a concurrent hopper. **We can explicitly defer fairness design** until concurrency > 1, and note the deferral. That's a free simplification the serial-MVP constraint gives us.
5. **Backoff?**
   - No backoff at MVP. Immediate retry. If the failure is flaky (MCP 500), immediate retry plausibly succeeds. If it's deterministic, immediate retry fails the same way and we exhaust the budget fast, which is what we want.

## The question I'd actually want to pull on

The interesting fork isn't the mechanics — it's **attempt-counting semantics** once we accept "writ = obligation, rig = attempt":

- Does `attempts` count all rigs, or just failed rigs? (I'd say all — it's a bound on cost, not on failures.)
- Does a patron-cancelled rig consume an attempt? (I'd say no — patron cancel is not a system failure.)
- When the combined brief→implementation rig becomes multi-rig later, does "attempts" transfer to individual rigs in the sequence, or stay at the writ level? (I'd say writ level, with per-rig attempt metadata as a separate concern.)

These are small decisions, but they set precedent for how the multi-rig refactor talks about cost and bounds.

## What I'd like from you

Two questions to start:

1. **Does the session-record-staleness signal feel sufficient**, or do you want engines to emit explicit heartbeats? (Explicit heartbeats = slightly more reliable, cost = every engine has to implement one, and we have to define the signal.)
2. **Retry default = 2 attempts total, immediate, no backoff** — comfortable with that as the shipping default, or want a more conservative "no auto-retry, escalate to human on first failure"?

If you want, I can also extend Vigil's entry in the vocabulary tome to explicitly cover stuck-rig detection as its MVP slice — the concept is already there, just not wired to this click.

#### c-mo3o28hb-a5fa7b0a95f9 [concluded]
> What does Patron Anima output — challenge questions, veto votes, annotations on proposed decisions, or something else?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Patron Anima emits, per decision:
- **verdict**: confirm / override / fill-in (mandatory; no abstain, no surface-flip)
- **selection**: picked from analyst's offered options only (no custom — human patron keeps that escape hatch; anima is deliberately more constrained for measurable, type-safe output)
- **confidence**: low / med / high (mandatory from day one — don't treat as optional or it'll get skipped)

Applied to every decision in the plan (both surfaced and auto-decided). Output shape is identical to existing patron-feedback shape, so it plugs into decision-review engine without new plumbing.

**Override rate tracked as a first-class planner-quality instrument.** Override × confidence gives two quasi-orthogonal signals:
- high override + high confidence → planner is weak on taste-relevant decisions
- high override + low confidence → anima is flailing
- high confirm + low confidence → anima doesn't know patron well enough
- high confirm + high confidence → system is working

**Low-confidence handling default: don't raise to patron.** Flip to 'raise if confidence < X' only if metrics show problems.

#### c-mo3o2bqi-1fb5f199f0d2 [concluded]
> How does Patron Anima handle self-uncertainty — when it doesn't know what Sean would want, does it guess, abstain, escalate, or something else?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Patron Anima self-uncertainty resolved along two surfaces, plus an authoring hint.

**Verdict-time (post-decision, forced output):** Default to confirm under uncertainty, mark low confidence. Analyst's pick is trusted; low-confidence confirms accumulate in the high-confirm/low-conf diagnostic quadrant ("anima doesn't know patron well enough"). No fallback meta-rules at verdict — if Sean has meta-preferences (e.g., prefer reversibility), they live in the principles list and get marked high-confidence through that principle, not smuggled in as quiet fallbacks.

**Interview-time (pre-plan via Distiller ask-patron):** Anima can abstain ("no signal on this"). Distiller owns downstream disposition — drop the question, route to Sean, or flag the gap in the brief. Asymmetry with verdict-time is deliberate: confident-wrong at interview-time shapes the whole plan rather than a single decision, so abstention is worth the cost of inconsistent output shape.

**Authoring hint — confidence calibration:** Principle-grounded calibration scheme for the role file — exactly one principle applies cleanly → high; multiple principles conflict → med (and note which conflict); no principle applies → low. Turns the anima's self-confidence from a vibes question into a structural check against the principles list. Belongs in the eventual authoring brief; collated via c-mo3o2cm3 conclusion.

Meta-rules under uncertainty as a *planner-level* strategy (prefer reversibility / simpler / smaller change when anima returns low confidence) spun out as a separate planner-strategy question — not an anima fallback.
Created: 2026-04-18T01:36:44.010Z
Resolved: 2026-04-18T11:50:28.994Z

#### c-mo3o2cm3-8cc5f4b44c10 [concluded]
> What is the MVP cut — the smallest version of Patron Anima that produces visible planner-quality value, and what is explicitly deferred?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: MVP Patron Anima = smallest version that emits per-decision verdicts plugging into the existing patron-feedback shape, backed by a single principles-list role file authored from Sean's sanctum artifacts.

## Shape
- Single role file (principles-list format), guild-specific, installed via Astrolabe config. No family of specialized variants.
- Skipped entirely when no patron role is configured. No framework default.

## Pipeline position
- Pre-plan: Distiller interviews via ask-patron tool during brief distillation; anima may abstain ("no signal").
- Post-plan: per-decision verdict on analyst's finalized plan.

## Per-decision output (post-plan)
- verdict: confirm | override | fill-in
- selection: one of analyst's offered options (no custom)
- confidence: low | med | high (mandatory)
- Default under uncertainty: confirm + low confidence.

## v1 corpus (authoring sources)
Concluded click conclusions, session notes, philosophy + guild-metaphor + CLAUDE files. Transcripts and higher-volume sources deferred.

## Authoring hints (for the eventual commission brief)
- Principles-list format matches how Sean articulates taste.
- Confidence calibration is principle-structural: 1 principle applies → high, conflict → med, none apply → low.
- Volume curation is the central authoring challenge (system prompt is finite context).

## Primary instrument
Override rate × confidence — 4-quadrant diagnostic:
- high override / high confidence → planner weak on taste-relevant decisions
- high override / low confidence → anima flailing
- high confirm / low confidence → anima doesn't know patron well enough
- high confirm / high confidence → system working

Freshness signaled by override-rate trend; refresh cadence is docs-maintenance.

## Explicitly deferred (→ c-mo3ueh8i post-MVP bucket)
- Family of specialized patron variants
- Mid-plan analyst-initiated consultation (c-mo3phwj6, parked)
- Socratic-interview corpus refinement (c-mo3u8ay0, parked) — v1 authors from artifacts only
- Expanded corpus sources: transcripts, commission log, coco log, git history, click-tree shape, ethnographer interviews, briefs drafts, patron plan-feedback, commission reviews
- Structured-by-decision-type role file
- Function-generated (dynamic) role instructions
- Planner meta-heuristics for low-confidence handling (c-mo49zsst, live)
Created: 2026-04-18T01:36:45.147Z
Resolved: 2026-04-18T11:59:29.342Z

#### c-mo3tivzf-3c0b6de9406b [concluded]
> When no patron role is configured in Astrolabe, should patron-anima steps be skipped entirely or should the framework ship a default patron?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Skip patron-anima steps when no patron role is configured. No framework default. Rationale: every patron has different taste by definition, so a framework default represents no patron's taste (incoherent, not neutral); a clicks-only default would be too thin without sanctum-authored sources; a quality-degraded default risks poisoning trust in the mechanism. Framework provides the configuration slot; patrons fill it to opt in. Pattern observation worth tracking: this opens adjacent questions about dynamically shaping rigs and prompts when pipeline features are optional.

### c-mo3qj9by-ed5ad14e41d6 [concluded]
> Define the shape of the clarified-feature-spec — required/optional sections, minimum content per section, field types, and validation rules for 'this spec is complete enough to hand to the analyst'
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Final shape of the clarified-feature-spec. Six sections total.

| Section | Tier | Structure |
|---|---|---|
| Summary | Required | Prose paragraph, ~500 chars, describes what the feature is and who it's for |
| Behaviors | Required (≥1) | List of {id: B1..., trigger, outcome, source} — uniform records regardless of flow type |
| Acceptance Criteria | Required (≥1) | List of {id: AC1..., statement, refs to behaviors} — each behavior must be covered by ≥1 AC |
| Out of Scope | Required-presence | List of explicit non-goals; can be 'nothing notable' but must be addressed |
| Edge Cases | Required-presence | **References-only** — list of {behavior-id, one-line rationale}; classification layer pointing into Behaviors |
| Open Questions | Required-presence | List of {question, context, confidence} or empty; forces BA self-check |

**Sections explicitly dropped from initial shape** (see sibling clicks for rationale):
- Dependencies (c-mo3r9a8s) — requires canon-research / feature catalog infrastructure we don't have
- Success Metrics (c-mo3r9auv) — aspirational; verification umbrella handles this downstream
- Use Cases (c-mo3rhkrh) — fabrication and scope-creep risk; gap filled by AC and Edge Cases

**Cross-cutting rules:**
- **Provenance tagging:** every item in Behaviors and optional-anchored positions must cite source (brief | extrapolated from [anchor] | interview Q[n]). Creates auditability and prevents fabrication.
- **User-observability test:** every outcome in Behaviors must be user-visible (BA domain). Implementation-visible-only changes belong to the analyst, not the BA.
- **Cross-section linking:** AC ids reference behavior ids; Edge Cases entries reference behavior ids. Stable identifiers enable machine-checkable coverage.

**Validation rule for 'complete enough to hand to analyst':**
- All required sections non-empty with real content (not placeholders)
- ≥1 behavior and ≥1 AC
- Every behavior covered by ≥1 AC
- Required-presence sections addressed (can be 'none' but must say so)

**Governing principle** (inherited from the broader BA/PO stage role split): BA resolves product-layer ambiguity; reader/analyst resolves implementation ambiguity. Sections that invite speculation beyond the BA's accessible sources (brief, referenced material, interview) were trimmed.
Created: 2026-04-18T02:45:53.278Z
Resolved: 2026-04-18T03:24:58.263Z

#### c-mo3r9a8s-1d9bb8e02477 [concluded]
> Dependencies section — why excluded from initial shape and when to revisit
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Excluded from initial feature-spec shape. The BA has no reliable source for product-level system knowledge beyond what's explicitly named in the brief. The three sources the BA can reach — brief text, referenced material (concluded clicks/docs the brief links), Patron Anima interview — don't include a queryable feature catalog. The anima is a judgment layer for proposed content, not a retrieval layer for existing content; asking it to enumerate related features invites fabrication from implicit training knowledge.

Revisit when canon-research (c-mo3d6pkh) or atlas (c-mo1mqhql) lands. At that point either (a) canon-research populates Dependencies before BA runs, or (b) BA queries canon-research during its pass. Until then, dependencies named in the brief are visible to the analyst directly; a dedicated section is redundant and risky.

#### c-mo3r9auv-7124ed1f0aa7 [concluded]
> Success Metrics section — why excluded from initial shape and when to revisit
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Excluded from initial feature-spec shape. Metrics are inherently aspirational/future-state — the BA has no way to validate them or tie them to real measurement infrastructure. Risk of aspirational fabrication is high: the BA can produce plausible-sounding metrics that nobody tracks, creating false signal for the analyst (implying success criteria beyond Acceptance Criteria) and inviting scope creep.

Revisit after the verification umbrella (c-mo3dj3u3) lands. Verification work will produce real measurement infrastructure and define what 'working in production' looks like structurally. Once that exists, Success Metrics as a feature-spec section becomes grounded in concrete instrumentation rather than speculation.

207	> One anima or a family — is Patron Anima a single role applied to every decision, or do we spawn specialized variants (Patron-on-architecture, Patron-on-ergonomics, etc.)?
208	Status: concluded
209	Conclusion: Single patron role for MVP; family architecture (multiple role files + routing) deferred until forcing functions surface. Sean's taste across domains is largely unified principles applied to different surfaces — principles-list format captures this naturally. Per-domain diagnostic slicing achievable via decision-level domain tags (see related click) rather than splitting the patron. Surface-specific texture (TS idioms, CLI ergonomics) lives in CLAUDE and convention docs that feed implementers directly, not a decision anima. 213	#### c-mo3o2bqi-1fb5f199f0d2 [concluded]
214	> How does Patron Anima handle self-uncertainty — when it doesn't know what Sean would want, does it guess, abstain, escalate, or something else?
215	Status: concluded
216	Conclusion: Patron Anima self-uncertainty resolved along two surfaces, plus an authoring hint.
217	
218	**Verdict-time (post-decision, forced output):** Default to confirm under uncertainty, mark low confidence. Analyst's pick is trusted; low-confidence confirms accumulate in the high-confirm/low-conf diagnostic quadrant ("anima doesn't know patron well enough"). No fallback meta-rules at verdict — if Sean has meta-preferences (e.g., prefer reversibility), they live in the principles list and get marked high-confidence through that principle, not smuggled in as quiet fallbacks.
219	
220	**Interview-time (pre-plan via Distiller ask-patron):** Anima can abstain ("no signal on this"). Distiller owns downstream disposition — drop the question, route to Sean, or flag the gap in the brief. Asymmetry with verdict-time is deliberate: confident-wrong at interview-time shapes the whole plan rather than a single decision, so abstention is worth the cost of inconsistent output shape.
221	
222	**Authoring hint — confidence calibration:** Principle-grounded calibration scheme for the role file — exactly one principle applies cleanly → high; multiple principles conflict → med (and note which conflict); no principle applies → low. Turns the anima's self-confidence from a vibes question into a structural check against the principles list. Belongs in the eventual authoring brief; collated via c-mo3o2cm3 conclusion.
223	
224	Meta-rules under uncertainty as a *planner-level* strategy (prefer reversibility / simpler / smaller change when anima returns low confidence) spun out as a separate planner-strategy question — not an anima fallback.
225	Created: 2026-04-18T01:36:44.010Z
226	Resolved: 2026-04-18T11:50:28.994Z

228	#### c-mo3o2cm3-8cc5f4b44c10 [concluded]
229	> What is the MVP cut — the smallest version of Patron Anima that produces visible planner-quality value, and what is explicitly deferred?
230	Status: concluded
231	Conclusion: MVP Patron Anima = smallest version that emits per-decision verdicts plugging into the existing patron-feedback shape, backed by a single principles-list role file authored from Sean's sanctum artifacts.
232	
233	## Shape
234	- Single role file (principles-list format), guild-specific, installed via Astrolabe config. No family of specialized variants.
235	- Skipped entirely when no patron role is configured. No framework default.
236	
237	## Pipeline position
238	- Pre-plan: Distiller interviews via ask-patron tool during brief distillation; anima may abstain ("no signal").
239	- Post-plan: per-decision verdict on analyst's finalized plan.
240	
241	## Per-decision output (post-plan)
242	- verdict: confirm | override | fill-in
243	- selection: one of analyst's offered options (no custom)
244	- confidence: low | med | high (mandatory)
245	- Default under uncertainty: confirm + low confidence.
246	
247	## v1 corpus (authoring sources)
248	Concluded click conclusions, session notes, philosophy + guild-metaphor + CLAUDE files. Transcripts and higher-volume sources deferred.
249	
250	## Authoring hints (for the eventual commission brief)
251	- Principles-list format matches how Sean articulates taste.
252	- Confidence calibration is principle-structural: 1 principle applies → high, conflict → med, none apply → low.
253	- Volume curation is the central authoring challenge (system prompt is finite context).
254	
255	## Primary instrument
256	Override rate × confidence — 4-quadrant diagnostic:
257	- high override / high confidence → planner weak on taste-relevant decisions
258	- high override / low confidence → anima flailing
259	- high confirm / low confidence → anima doesn't know patron well enough
260	- high confirm / high confidence → system working
261	
262	Freshness signaled by override-rate trend; refresh cadence is docs-maintenance.
263	
264	## Explicitly deferred (→ c-mo3ueh8i post-MVP bucket)
265	- Family of specialized patron variants
266	- Mid-plan analyst-initiated consultation (c-mo3phwj6, parked)
267	- Socratic-interview corpus refinement (c-mo3u8ay0, parked) — v1 authors from artifacts only
268	- Expanded corpus sources: transcripts, commission log, coco log, git history, click-tree shape, ethnographer interviews, briefs drafts, patron plan-feedback, commission reviews
269	- Structured-by-decision-type role file
270	- Function-generated (dynamic) role instructions
271	- Planner meta-heuristics for low-confidence handling (c-mo49zsst, live)
272	Created: 2026-04-18T01:36:45.147Z
273	Resolved: 2026-04-18T11:59:29.342Z
274	
275	#### c-mo3tivzf-3c0b6de9406b [concluded]
276	> When no patron role is configured in Astrolabe, should patron-anima steps be skipped entirely or should the framework ship a default patron?
277	Status: concluded
278	Conclusion: Skip patron-anima steps when no patron role is configured. No framework default. Rationale: every patron has different taste by definition, so a framework default represents no patron's taste (incoherent, not neutral); a clicks-only default would be too thin without sanctum-authored sources; a quality-degraded default risks poisoning trust in the mechanism. Framework provides the configuration slot; patrons fill it to opt in. Pattern observation worth tracking: this opens adjacent questions about dynamically shaping rigs and prompts when pipeline features are optional.
279	Created: 2026-04-18T04:09:34.827Z
280	Resolved: 2026-04-18T04:09:42.649Z

Required for the Distiller↔Patron interview contract to have a clean refusal path.

## c-mo3d6trd-ffe48390886f [concluded]
> Design a Patron Anima — socratically-trained standin that carries patron taste and values for decisions without clean objective answers
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Patron Anima design complete for v1; MVP commissioned to nexus as writ w-mo4bke5x-0a1d273bbb19 (complexity 5).

Final MVP shape (authoritative detail in c-mo3o2cm3):
- Framework engine slotting between inventory-check and decision-review in Astrolabe's plan-and-ship rig template
- Astrolabe config variable (patronRole) as the extension point; engine no-ops when unset
- Per-decision patron emission: verdict (confirm/override/fill-in) + selection + confidence
- Single-session single-pass; malformed/missing output falls through to decision-review for human handling
- Per-Decision patron section on PlanDoc carries verdict + confidence for override-rate × confidence instrumentation
- Skip-when-unset behavior; no framework default patron

Design subtree explored 7 children of the scope click (c-mo3o0euk) covering corpus, pipeline position, output shape, one-vs-family, self-uncertainty handling, default-patron policy, and MVP cut. Post-MVP bucket (c-mo3ueh8i) carries forward deferred features (mid-plan consultation, expanded corpus sources, specialized patron variants, socratic interview, per-decision sessions, reporting surface for override-rate).

Resolved: 2026-04-18T12:35:18.730Z

### c-mo3o0euk-054bbde3ae5f [concluded]
> Scope the MVP Patron Anima — what's in the first prototype, what's deferred, and what design questions do we need to answer before commissioning it?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: MVP Patron Anima scoped across 7 children. Collated shape lives in c-mo3o2cm3 conclusion (authoritative MVP cut). Design questions all resolved:

- Corpus (c-mo3o2a8h): input set for authoring a principles-list role file; v1 sources = concluded clicks, session notes, philosophy/guild-metaphor/CLAUDE.
- Family vs single (c-mo3o2b49): single role for MVP.
- Pipeline position (c-mo3o292o): pre-plan Distiller interview + post-plan verdict.
- Output shape (c-mo3o28hb): verdict + selection + confidence, mapped to existing patron-feedback shape.
- Self-uncertainty (c-mo3o2bqi): default-to-confirm at verdict-time, abstain at interview-time, principle-grounded confidence calibration.
- Default-patron handling (c-mo3tivzf): skip steps when no patron role configured; no framework default.
- MVP cut (c-mo3o2cm3): collator for all of the above.

Deferrals accumulated in c-mo3ueh8i post-MVP bucket. Next phase is commission authoring — harvest authoring hints from this subtree into a brief in .scratch/.

Resolved: 2026-04-18T11:59:46.766Z

#### c-mo3o28hb-a5fa7b0a95f9 [concluded]
> What does Patron Anima output — challenge questions, veto votes, annotations on proposed decisions, or something else?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Patron Anima emits, per decision:
- **verdict**: confirm / override / fill-in (mandatory; no abstain, no surface-flip)
- **selection**: picked from analyst's offered options only (no custom — human patron keeps that escape hatch; anima is deliberately more constrained for measurable, type-safe output)
- **confidence**: low / med / high (mandatory from day one — don't treat as optional or it'll get skipped)

Applied to every decision in the plan (both surfaced and auto-decided). Output shape is identical to existing patron-feedback shape, so it plugs into decision-review engine without new plumbing.

**Override rate tracked as a first-class planner-quality instrument.** Override × confidence gives two quasi-orthogonal signals:
- high override + high confidence → planner is weak on taste-relevant decisions
- high override + low confidence → anima is flailing
- high confirm + low confidence → anima doesn't know patron well enough
- high confirm + high confidence → system is working

**Low-confidence handling default: don't raise to patron.** Flip to 'raise if confidence < X' only if metrics show problems.
Created: 2026-04-18T01:36:39.791Z
Resolved: 2026-04-18T01:52:09.724Z

Mid-plan analyst-initiated consultation (c-mo3phwj6) deferred to post-MVP and reparented under the post-MVP bucket.

Future state worth tracking: role instructions produced by a function() rather than a static .md pointer (see related click c-mo3tiwy6).

#### c-mo3o2bqi-1fb5f199f0d2 [concluded]
> How does Patron Anima handle self-uncertainty — when it doesn't know what Sean would want, does it guess, abstain, escalate, or something else?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Patron Anima self-uncertainty resolved along two surfaces, plus an authoring hint.

**Verdict-time (post-decision, forced output):** Default to confirm under uncertainty, mark low confidence. Analyst's pick is trusted; low-confidence confirms accumulate in the high-confirm/low-conf diagnostic quadrant ("anima doesn't know patron well enough"). No fallback meta-rules at verdict — if Sean has meta-preferences (e.g., prefer reversibility), they live in the principles list and get marked high-confidence through that principle, not smuggled in as quiet fallbacks.

**Interview-time (pre-plan via Distiller ask-patron):** Anima can abstain ("no signal on this"). Distiller owns downstream disposition — drop the question, route to Sean, or flag the gap in the brief. Asymmetry with verdict-time is deliberate: confident-wrong at interview-time shapes the whole plan rather than a single decision, so abstention is worth the cost of inconsistent output shape.

**Authoring hint — confidence calibration:** Principle-grounded calibration scheme for the role file — exactly one principle applies cleanly → high; multiple principles conflict → med (and note which conflict); no principle applies → low. Turns the anima's self-confidence from a vibes question into a structural check against the principles list. Belongs in the eventual authoring brief; collated via c-mo3o2cm3 conclusion.

Meta-rules under uncertainty as a *planner-level* strategy (prefer reversibility / simpler / smaller change when anima returns low confidence) spun out as a separate planner-strategy question — not an anima fallback.
Created: 2026-04-18T01:36:44.010Z
Resolved: 2026-04-18T11:50:28.994Z

#### c-mo3o2cm3-8cc5f4b44c10 [concluded]
> What is the MVP cut — the smallest version of Patron Anima that produces visible planner-quality value, and what is explicitly deferred?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: MVP Patron Anima = smallest version that emits per-decision verdicts plugging into the existing patron-feedback shape, backed by a single principles-list role file authored from Sean's sanctum artifacts.

## Shape
- Single role file (principles-list format), guild-specific, installed via Astrolabe config. No family of specialized variants.
- Skipped entirely when no patron role is configured. No framework default.

## Pipeline position
- Pre-plan: Distiller interviews via ask-patron tool during brief distillation; anima may abstain ("no signal").
- Post-plan: per-decision verdict on analyst's finalized plan.

## Per-decision output (post-plan)
- verdict: confirm | override | fill-in
- selection: one of analyst's offered options (no custom)
- confidence: low | med | high (mandatory)
- Default under uncertainty: confirm + low confidence.

## v1 corpus (authoring sources)
Concluded click conclusions, session notes, philosophy + guild-metaphor + CLAUDE files. Transcripts and higher-volume sources deferred.

## Authoring hints (for the eventual commission brief)
- Principles-list format matches how Sean articulates taste.
- Confidence calibration is principle-structural: 1 principle applies → high, conflict → med, none apply → low.
- Volume curation is the central authoring challenge (system prompt is finite context).

## Primary instrument
Override rate × confidence — 4-quadrant diagnostic:
- high override / high confidence → planner weak on taste-relevant decisions
- high override / low confidence → anima flailing
- high confirm / low confidence → anima doesn't know patron well enough
- high confirm / high confidence → system working

Freshness signaled by override-rate trend; refresh cadence is docs-maintenance.

## Explicitly deferred (→ c-mo3ueh8i post-MVP bucket)
- Family of specialized patron variants
- Mid-plan analyst-initiated consultation (c-mo3phwj6, parked)
- Socratic-interview corpus refinement (c-mo3u8ay0, parked) — v1 authors from artifacts only
- Expanded corpus sources: transcripts, commission log, coco log, git history, click-tree shape, ethnographer interviews, briefs drafts, patron plan-feedback, commission reviews
- Structured-by-decision-type role file
- Function-generated (dynamic) role instructions
- Planner meta-heuristics for low-confidence handling (c-mo49zsst, live)
Created: 2026-04-18T01:36:45.147Z
Resolved: 2026-04-18T11:59:29.342Z

#### c-mo3tivzf-3c0b6de9406b [concluded]
> When no patron role is configured in Astrolabe, should patron-anima steps be skipped entirely or should the framework ship a default patron?
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Skip patron-anima steps when no patron role is configured. No framework default. Rationale: every patron has different taste by definition, so a framework default represents no patron's taste (incoherent, not neutral); a clicks-only default would be too thin without sanctum-authored sources; a quality-degraded default risks poisoning trust in the mechanism. Framework provides the configuration slot; patrons fill it to opt in. Pattern observation worth tracking: this opens adjacent questions about dynamically shaping rigs and prompts when pipeline features are optional.

content.text:
Honest reread: I was framing "MVP" narrowly as **c-mo28k3ar — the serial-hopper milestone**, which is literally titled "the MVP autonomy milestone." That click is scoped to execution mechanics. But that's not the same thing as "MVP autonomous operation" broadly, which is what you're probing.

Two orthogonal axes, both load-bearing:

1. **Execution mechanics** (serial-hopper milestone): can the system dispatch, detect stuck, retry, observe? Without this, nothing runs.
2. **Decision quality** (evidence-based decisional machinery): does the planner produce decisions the patron will ship without pre-review? Without enough of this, autonomous output is junk *even if* the pipeline runs.

My first answer only covered axis 1. That was a miss. But I'd still parse the decisional-machinery tree itself into MVP vs post-MVP rather than calling the whole thing in-scope:

## In-scope for MVP

- **Patron Anima MVP** — genuinely MVP-critical. Ratification-not-approval falls over without it: if there's no anima standin for your taste, the taste decisions either stall waiting for you or get made without signal. Already dispatched as w-mo4bke5x (sessions running, per the recent commits). I should have surfaced this in the first answer.
- **BA/PO pre-plan stage** — mixed. Some of it has shipped (sage-reader-analyst updates). The Open-Questions handler is live design and arguably MVP. The Distiller interview path intersects Patron Anima directly.

## Above-the-floor (post-MVP quality)

- Canon-research agent, external-research agent
- Experts panel
- Steelman advocates + arbiter
- Bold Agent / boldness budget
- Arbitrary-choice mechanism
- Ambiguity score
- Cost-weighted planning
- Planner meta-heuristics for low-confidence handling

The reason I'd put these post-MVP: the **existing razor + 3 codified defaults + 3.7% override baseline is already the quality floor**. That floor is shipping. Evidence-based machinery would sharpen it from "pretty good" to "robust on hard decisions" — real work, but not blockers for the first autonomous cycle. If the override rate blows up once the patron steps back, *that's* when these pieces earn their place, and we'll have empirical data to prioritize which ones.

## What this changes

- I should reframe the "five gates" answer from earlier: those are the five gates on the **execution axis**. The full MVP picture is execution gates + Patron Anima in flight.
- Patron Anima is currently the single most active MVP-critical work item, and it's already dispatched. So the decisional-quality axis isn't stuck waiting on us — it's actively being built.
- Does this shift what we pick up next? Stuck detection is still a true prereq on the execution path; it doesn't go away. But if you want to pivot to a decisional-quality click instead (e.g., Open-Questions handler, or the planner meta-heuristics question), the tree has room.

Want me to hold course on stuck detection, or pivot?

content: We're mapping the autonomous-operation click tree and picked stuck-rig detection plus failure/retry policy as the next design to crack, after clarifying that Patron Anima is also MVP-critical. Next: decide whether to hold course on stuck detection or pivot to a decision-quality click. (disable recaps in /config)

1	/**
2	 * The Animator — public types.
3	 *
4	 * These types form the contract between The Animator apparatus and all
5	 * callers (summon relay, nsg consult, etc.). No implementation details.
6	 *
7	 * See: docs/specification.md (animator)
8	 */
9	
10	import type { AnimaWeave } from '@shardworks/loom-apparatus';
11	import type { ResolvedTool } from '@shardworks/tools-apparatus';
12	
13	// ── Session chunks (streaming output) ────────────────────────────────
14	
15	/** A chunk of output from a running session. */
16	export type SessionChunk =
17	  | { type: 'text'; text: string }
18	  | { type: 'tool_use'; tool: string }
19	  | { type: 'tool_result'; tool: string };
20	
21	// ── Request / Result ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
22	
23	export interface AnimateRequest {
24	  /**
25	   * Optional pre-generated session id. When provided, the Animator uses
26	   * this id instead of generating a new one. Used by summon() to make the
27	   * session id available on the handle before the Loom weave resolves.
28	   */
29	  sessionId?: string;
30	  /** The anima weave from The Loom (composed identity context). */
31	  context: AnimaWeave;
32	  /**
33	   * The work prompt — what the anima should do.
34	   * Passed directly to the session provider as the initial prompt.
35	   * This bypasses The Loom — it is not a composition concern.
36	   */
37	  prompt?: string;
38	  /**
39	   * Working directory for the session.
40	   * The session provider launches the AI process here.
41	   */
42	  cwd: string;
43	  /**
44	   * Optional conversation id to resume a multi-turn conversation.
45	   * If provided, the session provider resumes the existing conversation
46	   * rather than starting a new one.
47	   */
48	  conversationId?: string;
49	  /**
50	   * Caller-supplied metadata recorded alongside the session.
51	   * The Animator stores this as-is — it does not interpret the contents.
52	   */
53	  metadata?: Record<string, unknown>;
54	  /**
55	   * Enable streaming output. When true, the returned `chunks` iterable
56	   * yields output as the session produces it. When false (default), the
57	   * `chunks` iterable completes immediately with no items.
58	   *
59	   * Either way, the return shape is the same: `{ chunks, result }`.
60	   */
61	  streaming?: boolean;
62	  /**
63	   * Task-layer environment variables. Overrides the identity-layer
64	   * environment from the AnimaWeave when keys collide. Spread into the
65	   * spawned process environment.
66	   */
67	  environment?: Record<string, string>;
68	}
69	
70	export interface SessionResult {
71	  /** Unique session id (generated by The Animator). */
72	  id: string;
73	  /** Terminal status. */
74	  status: 'completed' | 'failed' | 'timeout' | 'cancelled';
75	  /** When the session started (ISO-8601). */
76	  startedAt: string;
77	  /** When the session ended (ISO-8601). */
78	  endedAt: string;
79	  /** Wall-clock duration in milliseconds. */
80	  durationMs: number;
81	  /** Provider name (e.g. 'claude-code'). */
82	  provider: string;
83	  /** Numeric exit code from the provider process. */
84	  exitCode: number;
85	  /** Error message if failed. */
86	  error?: string;
87	  /** Conversation id (for multi-turn resume). */
88	  conversationId?: string;
89	  /** Session id from the provider (e.g. for --resume). */
90	  providerSessionId?: string;
91	  /** Token usage from the provider, if available. */
92	  tokenUsage?: TokenUsage;
93	  /** Cost in USD from the provider, if available. */
94	  costUsd?: number;
95	  /** Caller-supplied metadata, recorded as-is. */
96	  metadata?: Record<string, unknown>;
97	  /**
98	   * The final assistant text from the session.
99	   * Extracted by the Animator from the provider's transcript.
100	   * Useful for programmatic consumers that need the session's conclusion
101	   * without parsing the full transcript (e.g. the Spider's review collect step).
102	   */
103	  output?: string;
104	}
105	
106	export interface TokenUsage {
107	  inputTokens: number;
108	  outputTokens: number;
109	  cacheReadTokens?: number;
110	  cacheWriteTokens?: number;
111	}
112	
113	// ── Summon request ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
114	
115	export interface SummonRequest {
116	  /**
117	   * The work prompt — what the anima should do.
118	   * Passed directly to the session provider as the initial prompt.
119	   */
120	  prompt: string;
121	  /**
122	   * The role to summon (e.g. 'artificer', 'scribe').
123	   * Passed to The Loom for context composition and recorded in session metadata.
124	   */
125	  role?: string;
126	  /**
127	   * Working directory for the session.
128	   * The session provider launches the AI process here.
129	   */
130	  cwd: string;
131	  /**
132	   * Optional conversation id to resume a multi-turn conversation.
133	   */
134	  conversationId?: string;
135	  /**
136	   * Additional metadata to record alongside the session.
137	   * Merged with auto-generated metadata (trigger: 'summon', role).
138	   */
139	  metadata?: Record<string, unknown>;
140	  /**
141	   * Enable streaming output. When true, the returned `chunks` iterable
142	   * yields output as the session produces it. When false (default), the
143	   * `chunks` iterable completes immediately with no items.
144	   */
145	  streaming?: boolean;
146	  /**
147	   * Task-layer environment variables. Overrides the identity-layer
148	   * environment from the AnimaWeave when keys collide. Spread into the
149	   * spawned process environment.
150	   */
151	  environment?: Record<string, string>;
152	}
153	
154	// ── Animator API (the `provides` interface) ──────────────────────────
155	
156	/** The return value from animate() and summon(). */
157	export interface AnimateHandle {
158	  /**
159	   * Session ID, available immediately after launch — before the session
160	   * completes. Callers that only need to know the session was launched
161	   * (e.g. quick engines returning `{ status: 'launched', sessionId }`)
162	   * can return without awaiting `result`.
163	   */
164	  sessionId: string;
165	  /**
166	   * Async iterable of output chunks from the session. When streaming is
167	   * disabled (the default), this iterable completes immediately with no
168	   * items. When streaming is enabled, it yields chunks as the session
169	   * produces output.
170	   */
171	  chunks: AsyncIterable<SessionChunk>;
172	  /**
173	   * Promise that resolves to the final SessionResult after the session
174	   * completes (or fails/times out) and the result is recorded to The Stacks.
175	   */
176	  result: Promise<SessionResult>;
177	}
178	
179	export interface AnimatorApi {
180	  /**
181	   * Summon an anima — compose context via The Loom and launch a session.
182	   *
183	   * This is the high-level "make an anima do a thing" entry point.
184	   * Internally calls The Loom for context composition (passing the role),
185	   * then animate() for session launch and recording. The work prompt
186	   * bypasses the Loom and goes directly to the provider.
187	   *
188	   * Requires The Loom apparatus to be installed. Throws if not available.
189	   *
190	   * Auto-populates session metadata with `trigger: 'summon'` and `role`.
191	   *
192	   * Returns synchronously — the async work lives inside `result` and `chunks`.
193	   */
194	  summon(request: SummonRequest): AnimateHandle;
195	
196	  /**
197	   * Animate a session — launch an AI process with the given context.
198	   *
199	   * This is the low-level entry point for callers that compose their own
200	   * AnimaWeave (e.g. The Parlour for multi-turn conversations).
201	   *
202	   * Records the session result to The Stacks before `result` resolves.
203	   *
204	   * Set `streaming: true` on the request to receive output chunks as the
205	   * session runs. When streaming is disabled (default), the `chunks`
206	   * iterable completes immediately with no items.
207	   *
208	   * Returns synchronously — the async work lives inside `result` and `chunks`.
209	   */
210	  animate(request: AnimateRequest): AnimateHandle;
211	
212	  /**
213	   * Subscribe to a running session's output stream.
214	   *
215	   * Returns an AsyncIterable that replays all chunks already produced
216	   * (from session start) and then yields new chunks as they arrive.
217	   * Returns null if there is no active in-memory session with the given id
218	   * (i.e. the session is unknown, already completed, or started before this
219	   * process started).
220	   *
221	   * When the session ends, the iterable completes automatically.
222	   */
223	  subscribeToSession(sessionId: string): AsyncIterable<SessionChunk> | null;
224	
225	  /**
226	   * Cancel a running session.
227	   *
228	   * Patches the SessionDoc to 'cancelled' with endedAt, durationMs, and
229	   * the optional reason in the error field. If cancelHandle is available,
230	   * delegates to the provider's cancel() method to kill the process.
231	   *
232	   * Idempotent: if the session is already in a terminal state, returns the
233	   * existing SessionDoc without modification. Throws if the session ID
234	   * does not exist.
235	   *
236	   * Does NOT wait for the process to die — returns immediately after
237	   * patching and sending the kill signal.
238	   */
239	  cancel(sessionId: string, options?: { reason?: string }): Promise<SessionDoc>;
240	}
241	
242	// ── Session provider interface ───────────────────────────────────────
243	
244	/**
245	 * A session provider — pluggable backend that knows how to launch and
246	 * communicate with a specific AI system.
247	 *
248	 * Implemented as an apparatus plugin whose `provides` object satisfies
249	 * this interface. The Animator discovers the provider via guild config:
250	 * `guild.json["animator"]["sessionProvider"]` names the plugin id.
251	 *
252	 * The provider always returns `{ chunks, result }` — the same shape as
253	 * AnimateHandle. When `config.streaming` is true, the provider MAY yield
254	 * output chunks as the session runs. When false (or when the provider
255	 * does not support streaming), the chunks iterable completes immediately
256	 * with no items. The Animator does not branch on streaming capability —
257	 * it passes the flag through and trusts the provider to do the right thing.
258	 */
259	export interface AnimatorSessionProvider {
260	  /** Human-readable name (e.g. 'claude-code'). */
261	  name: string;
262	
263	  /**
264	   * Launch a session. Returns `{ chunks, result }` synchronously.
265	   *
266	   * The `result` promise resolves when the AI process exits.
267	   * The `chunks` async iterable yields output when `config.streaming`
268	   * is true and the provider supports streaming; otherwise it completes
269	   * immediately with no items.
270	   *
271	   * Providers that don't support streaming simply ignore the flag and
272	   * return empty chunks — no separate method needed.
273	   */
274	  launch(config: SessionProviderConfig): {
275	    chunks: AsyncIterable<SessionChunk>;
276	    result: Promise<SessionProviderResult>;
277	    /**
278	     * Provider-specific process metadata for cross-process cancellation.
279	     * Resolves as soon as the process is spawned. The Animator persists the
280	     * resolved value as `cancelHandle` on the SessionDoc.
281	     *
282	     * Optional — providers that don't support cancellation omit this.
283	     */
284	    processInfo?: Promise<Record<string, unknown>>;
285	  };
286	
287	  /**
288	   * Cancel a running session using previously persisted process metadata.
289	   *
290	   * Called by the Animator with the cancelMetadata from the SessionDoc.
291	   * The provider interprets the metadata and kills the process.
292	   *
293	   * Optional — providers that don't support cancellation omit this.
294	   */
295	  cancel?(cancelMetadata: Record<string, unknown>): Promise<void>;
296	}
297	
298	export interface SessionProviderConfig {
299	  /**
300	   * Pre-generated session id. The Animator generates this before calling
301	   * launch() so both sides share the same id. Providers that spawn detached
302	   * processes need this to correlate lifecycle events (session-running,
303	   * session-record) back to the Animator's session record.
304	   */
305	  sessionId: string;
306	  /** System prompt for the AI process. May be undefined if composition is not yet implemented. */
307	  systemPrompt?: string;
308	  /** Initial user message (e.g. writ description). */
309	  initialPrompt?: string;
310	  /** Model to use (from guild settings). */
311	  model: string;
312	  /** Optional conversation id for resume. */
313	  conversationId?: string;
314	  /** Working directory for the session. */
315	  cwd: string;
316	  /**
317	   * Enable streaming output. When true, the provider should yield output
318	   * chunks as the session produces them. When false (default), the chunks
319	   * iterable should complete immediately with no items.
320	   *
321	   * Providers that don't support streaming may ignore this flag.
322	   */
323	  streaming?: boolean;
324	  /**
325	   * Resolved tools for this session. When present, the provider should
326	   * configure an MCP server with these tool definitions.
327	   *
328	   * The Loom resolves role → permissions → tools via the Instrumentarium.
329	   * The Animator passes them through from the AnimaWeave.
330	   */
331	  tools?: ResolvedTool[];
332	  /**
333	   * Merged environment variables to spread into the spawned process.
334	   * The Animator merges identity-layer (weave) and task-layer (request)
335	   * variables before passing them here — task layer wins on collision.
336	   */
337	  environment?: Record<string, string>;
338	}
339	
340	/** A single message from the NDJSON stream. Untyped — shape varies by provider. */
341	export type TranscriptMessage = Record<string, unknown>;
342	
343	export interface SessionProviderResult {
344	  /** Exit status. */
345	  status: 'completed' | 'failed' | 'timeout' | 'cancelled';
346	  /** Numeric exit code from the process. */
347	  exitCode: number;
348	  /** Error message if failed. */
349	  error?: string;
350	  /** Provider's session id (e.g. for --resume). */
351	  providerSessionId?: string;
352	  /** Token usage, if the provider can report it. */
353	  tokenUsage?: TokenUsage;
354	  /** Cost in USD, if the provider can report it. */
355	  costUsd?: number;
356	  /**
357	   * The session's full transcript — array of NDJSON message objects.
358	   *
359	   * In attached mode, this is populated directly from the NDJSON stream.
360	   * In detached mode, the transcript is written to SQLite by the babysitter
361	   * and is available via `stacks.book('animator', 'transcripts').get(sessionId)`
362	   * rather than on this result object.
363	   */
364	  transcript?: TranscriptMessage[];
365	  /**
366	   * The final assistant text from the session.
367	   * Extracted from the last assistant message's text content blocks.
368	   * Undefined if the session produced no assistant output.
369	   */
370	  output?: string;
371	  /** Process signal name if the session was killed by signal (e.g. 'SIGTERM', 'SIGKILL'). */
372	  signal?: string;
373	}
374	
375	// ── Stacks document type ─────────────────────────────────────────────
376	
377	/**
378	 * The session document stored in The Stacks' `sessions` book.
379	 * Includes all SessionResult fields plus the `id` required by BookEntry.
380	 */
381	export interface SessionDoc {
382	  id: string;
383	  /**
384	   * Session status. Initially written as `'running'` when the session is
385	   * launched, then updated to a terminal status (`'completed'`, `'failed'`,
386	   * `'timeout'`, or `'cancelled'`) after the provider exits or cancel is called.
387	   * The `'running'` state is transient.
388	   * `SessionResult.status` only includes terminal states.
389	   */
390	  status: 'pending' | 'running' | 'completed' | 'failed' | 'timeout' | 'cancelled';
391	  startedAt: string;
392	  endedAt?: string;
393	  durationMs?: number;
394	  provider: string;
395	  exitCode?: number;
396	  error?: string;
397	  conversationId?: string;
398	  providerSessionId?: string;
399	  tokenUsage?: TokenUsage;
400	  costUsd?: number;
401	  metadata?: Record<string, unknown>;
402	  /** The final assistant text from the session. */
403	  output?: string;
404	  /**
405	   * Provider-owned opaque handle for cross-process cancellation.
406	   * Written by the Animator from the provider's processInfo at session launch.
407	   * The Animator does not interpret this — it passes it back to the provider's
408	   * cancel() method when cancellation is requested.
409	   *
410	   * Shape is tagged by host type:
411	   * - local process: { kind: 'local-pgid', pgid: number }
412	   * - future container: { kind: 'container', containerId: string }
413	   * - future remote: { kind: 'remote', jobId: string, host: string }
414	   */
415	  cancelHandle?: Record<string, unknown>;
416	  /**
417	   * ISO timestamp of the last lifecycle signal from the session host.
418	   *
419	   * Updated on: pending pre-write, ready report (session-running),
420	   * heartbeat, terminal report (session-record). The guild writes its
421	   * own wall-clock time — never a host-supplied timestamp.
422	   *
423	   * The reconciler uses this to detect dead sessions:
424	   *   if (now - lastActivityAt - downtimeCredit > stalenessThreshold) → failed
425	   *
426	   * See: docs/architecture/detached-sessions.md § Reconciliation
427	   */
428	  lastActivityAt?: string;
429	  /**
430	   * Tool names this session is authorized to call over the Tool HTTP API.
431	   *
432	   * Source of truth for the daemon's Stacks-backed authorize callback.
433	   * Pre-written by `launchDetached()` (or animate()) before the babysitter
434	   * is spawned, then preserved through subsequent updates.
435	   */
436	  authorizedTools?: string[];
437	  /** Index signature required by BookEntry. */
438	  [key: string]: unknown;
439	}
440	
441	/**
442	 * The transcript document stored in The Stacks' `transcripts` book.
443	 * One record per session — 1:1 relationship with SessionDoc.
444	 */
445	export interface TranscriptDoc {
446	  /** Same as the session id. */
447	  id: string;
448	  /** Full NDJSON transcript from the session. */
449	  messages: TranscriptMessage[];
450	  /** Index signature required by BookEntry. */
451	  [key: string]: unknown;
452	}
453	
454	/**
455	 * Operational state stored in the Animator's 'state' book.
456	 * Single well-known document with id 'guild-heartbeat'.
457	 */
458	export interface GuildStateDoc {
459	  id: string;
460	  /** ISO timestamp of the last guild self-heartbeat. */
461	  guildAliveAt: string;
462	  /** Index signature required by BookEntry. */
463	  [key: string]: unknown;
464	}
465	
466	// ── Animator config ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
467	
468	/** Plugin configuration stored at guild.json["animator"]. */
469	export interface AnimatorConfig {
470	  /**
471	   * Plugin id of the apparatus that implements AnimatorSessionProvider.
472	   * The Animator looks this up via guild().apparatus() at animate-time.
473	   * Defaults to 'claude-code' if not specified.
474	   */
475	  sessionProvider?: string;
476	}
477	
478	// Augment GuildConfig so `guild().guildConfig().animator` is typed without
479	// requiring a manual type parameter at the call site.
480	declare module '@shardworks/nexus-core' {
481	  interface GuildConfig {
482	    animator?: AnimatorConfig;
483	  }
484	}
485
