content.content:
1	/**
2	 * The Spider — public types.
3	 *
4	 * Rig and engine data model, CrawlResult, SpiderApi, and configuration.
5	 * Engine yield shapes (DraftYields, SealYields) live here too so downstream
6	 * packages can import them without depending on the engine implementation files.
7	 */
8	
9	import type { ZodSchema } from 'zod';
10	
11	// ── Engine instance status ────────────────────────────────────────────
12	
13	export type EngineStatus = 'pending' | 'running' | 'completed' | 'failed' | 'cancelled' | 'blocked' | 'skipped';
14	
15	// ── Block record ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
16	
17	/**
18	 * Persisted record of an active engine block.
19	 * Present on an EngineInstance when status === 'blocked'.
20	 * Cleared when the block is resolved.
21	 */
22	export interface BlockRecord {
23	  /** Block type identifier (matches a registered BlockType.id). */
24	  type: string;
25	  /** Structured condition payload — shape validated by the block type's conditionSchema. */
26	  condition: unknown;
27	  /** ISO timestamp when the engine was blocked. */
28	  blockedAt: string;
29	  /** Optional human-readable message from the engine. */
30	  message?: string;
31	  /** ISO timestamp of the last checker evaluation. Updated on every check cycle. */
32	  lastCheckedAt?: string;
33	}
34	
35	// ── Engine instance ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
36	
37	/**
38	 * A single engine slot within a rig.
39	 *
40	 * `id` is the engine's position identifier (e.g. 'draft', 'implement').
41	 * For the static pipeline it matches `designId`.
42	 *
43	 * `givensSpec` holds values set at spawn time (writ, role, commands) and
44	 * may contain unresolved yield expression strings (`${yields.<id>.<path>}`)
45	 * that the Spider resolves at run time from upstream engine yields.
46	 */
47	export interface EngineInstance {
48	  /** Unique identifier within the rig (e.g. 'draft', 'implement'). */
49	  id: string;
50	  /** The engine design to look up in the Fabricator. */
51	  designId: string;
52	  /** Current execution status. */
53	  status: EngineStatus;
54	  /** Engine IDs that must be completed before this engine can run. */
55	  upstream: string[];
56	  /**
57	   * Givens values. Spawn-time expressions (`${writ}`, `${writ.*}`, `${vars.*}`)
58	   * are resolved to their values. Yield expressions (`${yields.*}`) remain as
59	   * literal `${yields.*}` strings and are resolved at run time when the engine
60	   * is executed.
61	   */
62	  givensSpec: Record<string, unknown>;
63	  /**
64	   * Conditional activation expression, copied from the template.
65	   * Evaluated at runtime when upstream is all done. Absent means unconditional.
66	   */
67	  when?: string;
68	  /** Yields from a completed engine run (JSON-serializable). */
69	  yields?: unknown;
70	  /** Error message if this engine failed. */
71	  error?: string;
72	  /** Session ID from a launched quick engine, used by the collect step. */
73	  sessionId?: string;
74	  /** ISO timestamp when execution started. */
75	  startedAt?: string;
76	  /** ISO timestamp when execution completed (or failed). */
77	  completedAt?: string;
78	  /** Present when status === 'blocked'. Cleared when the block is resolved. */
79	  block?: BlockRecord;
80	}
81	
82	// ── Rig ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
83	
84	export type RigStatus = 'running' | 'stuck' | 'completed' | 'failed' | 'cancelled' | 'blocked';
85	
86	/**
87	 * A rig — the execution context for a single writ.
88	 *
89	 * Stored in The Stacks (`spider/rigs` book). The `engines` array is the
90	 * ordered pipeline of engine instances. The Spider updates this document
91	 * in-place as engines run and complete.
92	 */
93	export interface RigDoc {
94	  /** Index signature required to satisfy BookEntry constraint. */
95	  [key: string]: unknown;
96	  /** Unique rig id. */
97	  id: string;
98	  /** The writ this rig is executing. */
99	  writId: string;
100	  /** Current rig status. */
101	  status: RigStatus;
102	  /** Ordered engine pipeline. */
103	  engines: EngineInstance[];
104	  /** ISO timestamp when the rig was created. */
105	  createdAt: string;
106	  /** Engine id whose yields provide the resolution summary. Set at spawn time. */
107	  resolutionEngineId?: string;
108	}
109	
110	// ── Rig filters ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
111	
112	/**
113	 * Filters for listing rigs.
114	 */
115	export interface RigFilters {
116	  /** Filter by rig status. */
117	  status?: RigStatus;
118	  /** Maximum number of results (default: 20). */
119	  limit?: number;
120	  /** Number of results to skip. */
121	  offset?: number;
122	}
123	
124	// ── Rig templates ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
125	
126	/**
127	 * A single engine slot declared in a rig template.
128	 */
129	export interface RigTemplateEngine {
130	  /** Engine id unique within this template. */
131	  id: string;
132	  /** Engine design id to look up in the Fabricator. */
133	  designId: string;
134	  /** Engine ids within this template whose completion is required first. Defaults to []. */
135	  upstream?: string[];
136	  /**
137	   * Givens to pass to the engine.
138	   *
139	   * String values may contain `${...}` template expressions:
140	   *   `${writ}` — the full WritDoc for this rig's writ
141	   *   `${writ.<path>}` — a field of the WritDoc (dot-path traversal)
142	   *   `${vars.<path>}` — value from spider.variables config (dot-path traversal)
143	   *   `${yields.<engine_id>.<path>}` — a property from an upstream engine's
144	   *       yields (resolved at run time, dot-path traversal)
145	   *
146	   * When a string is exactly one expression (e.g. `${writ}`), the resolved
147	   * value preserves its original type. When expressions are embedded in a
148	   * larger string, the result is always a string.
149	   *
150	   * Non-string values are passed through literally.
151	   * Whole-value expressions that resolve to undefined cause the key to be omitted.
152	   * Inline expressions that resolve to undefined are replaced with empty string.
153	   *
154	   * Use `\${` to produce a literal `${` in the output without interpolation.
155	   */
156	  givens?: Record<string, unknown>;
157	  /**
158	   * Conditional activation expression. A `${yields.<engine_id>.<property>}` reference
159	   * (with optional `!` negation prefix) evaluated at runtime when the engine's upstream
160	   * is all done. When the condition is falsy, the engine is set to `skipped` status.
161	   * When absent, the engine is unconditional (always runs).
162	   *
163	   * Examples:
164	   *   '${yields.review.passed}'  — run this engine when review.passed is truthy
165	   *   '!${yields.review.passed}' — run this engine when review.passed is falsy
166	   */
167	  when?: string;
168	}
169	
170	/**
171	 * A complete rig template.
172	 */
173	export interface RigTemplate {
174	  /** Ordered list of engine slot declarations. */
175	  engines: RigTemplateEngine[];
176	  /**
177	   * Engine id whose yields provide the writ resolution summary.
178	   * Falls back to seal engine, then last completed engine in array order.
179	   */
180	  resolutionEngine?: string;
181	}
182	
183	// ── CrawlResult ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
184	
185	/**
186	 * The result of a single crawl() call.
187	 *
188	 * Variants, ordered by priority:
189	 * - 'engine-completed'  — an engine finished (collected or ran inline); rig still running
190	 * - 'engine-started'    — launched a quick engine's session
191	 * - 'engine-blocked'    — engine entered blocked status; rig is still running (other engines active)
192	 * - 'engine-unblocked'  — a blocked engine's condition cleared; engine returned to pending
193	 * - 'rig-spawned'       — created a new rig for a ready writ
194	 * - 'rig-completed'     — the crawl step caused a rig to reach a terminal state
195	 * - 'rig-blocked'       — all forward progress stalled; rig entered blocked status
196	 * - 'gated'             — an open writ's dispatch is gated on outbound
197	 *                         spider.follows links that point at non-terminal
198	 *                         blockers; no rig was spawned and no status was
199	 *                         written (gate-only state is never persisted).
200	 * - 'writ-unstuck'      — a writ that Spider previously stuck via the gating

content.content:
600	// ── Spider-extended engine run / collect result types ─────────────────
601	
602	/**
603	 * Spider-extended engine run result. Adds an optional `graft` field
604	 * to the `completed` variant, allowing engines to dynamically append
605	 * new engines to the rig alongside their yields.
606	 *
607	 * Engines that want to graft import this type from @shardworks/spider-apparatus.
608	 * Engines that don't graft use the base EngineRunResult from @shardworks/fabricator-apparatus.
609	 *
610	 * The Spider internally checks for the `graft` property on any completed result
611	 * (duck-typing — the Fabricator type is not modified).
612	 */
613	export type SpiderEngineRunResult =
614	  | { status: 'completed'; yields: unknown; graft?: RigTemplateEngine[]; graftTail?: string }
615	  | { status: 'launched'; sessionId: string }
616	  | { status: 'blocked'; blockType: string; condition: unknown; message?: string };
617	
618	/**
619	 * Spider-extended collect result. When a quick engine's collect() method
620	 * returns an object with a `graft` property (an array), the Spider extracts
621	 * it as a graft request and uses the `yields` property as the engine's yields.
622	 *
623	 * When collect() returns a value without a `graft` array property, the entire
624	 * return value is treated as yields (backward compatible).
625	 */
626	export interface SpiderCollectResult {
627	  yields: unknown;
628	  graft?: RigTemplateEngine[];
629	  graftTail?: string;
630	}
631	
632	// ── status.spider sub-slot shape ────────────────────────────────────────
633	
634	/**
635	 * The reason a writ is stuck in Spider's gating machinery.
636	 *
637	 * - 'failed-blocker' — at least one outbound `spider.follows` blocker
638	 *                      reached `failed`; the dependent was cascaded to
639	 *                      `stuck` directly (not transitively).
640	 * - 'cycle'          — a back-edge was discovered in the `spider.follows`
641	 *                      graph during gate evaluation; every cycle member is
642	 *                      stuck with this cause.
643	 */
644	export type SpiderStuckCause = 'failed-blocker' | 'cycle';
645	
646	/**
647	 * Shape of the plugin-owned `status.spider` sub-slot as written by the
648	 * Spider's gating paths.
649	 *
650	 * The slot is absent (not the empty object) on writs Spider has never
651	 * touched. It is **also** absent on writs that reached `stuck` via the
652	 * pre-existing engine-cascade path (rig → writ CDC handler) — absence is
653	 * the load-bearing signal that tells `autoUnstick` not to revisit those
654	 * writs.
655	 *
656	 * The slot is written only on stuck transitions and cleared on
657	 * auto-unstick. Nothing is written while a writ is gated-but-not-stuck.
658	 */
659	export interface SpiderWritStatus {
660	  /** Present only while the writ is stuck for a gating reason. */
661	  stuckCause?: SpiderStuckCause;
662	  /**
663	   * The blockers responsible for the stuck transition. For
664	   * `failed-blocker`, these are the outbound `spider.follows` targets that
665	   * reached `failed`. For `cycle`, these are the members of the detected
666	   * cycle (typically including the dependent itself).
667	   */
668	  blockerIds?: string[];
669	  /** ISO timestamp recorded at the moment the stuck transition was taken. */

1	/**
2	 * Clerk public types.
3	 *
4	 * All types exported from @shardworks/clerk-apparatus.
5	 */
6	
7	// ── Writ phase ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
8	
9	/**
10	 * A writ's position in its lifecycle.
11	 *
12	 * Transitions:
13	 *   new    → open       (publish)   — draft enters the queue
14	 *   new    → cancelled  (cancel)
15	 *   open   → completed  (complete)
16	 *   open   → failed     (fail)
17	 *   open   → cancelled  (cancel)
18	 *   open   → stuck      (engine failure cascade)
19	 *   stuck  → open       (recovery/retry resumes execution)
20	 *   stuck  → failed     (obligation abandoned)
21	 *   stuck  → cancelled  (obligation withdrawn)
22	 *
23	 * completed, failed, cancelled are terminal — no further transitions.
24	 * stuck is non-terminal — it represents a "needs attention" state.
25	 */
26	export type WritPhase = 'new' | 'open' | 'stuck' | 'completed' | 'failed' | 'cancelled';
27	
28	// ── Documents ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
29	
30	/**
31	 * A writ document as stored in The Stacks.
32	 */
33	export interface WritDoc {
34	  /** Index signature required to satisfy BookEntry constraint. */
35	  [key: string]: unknown;
36	  /** Unique writ id (`w-{base36_timestamp}{hex_random}`). Sortable by creation time. */
37	  id: string;
38	  /** Writ type — must be a type declared in guild config, or a built-in type. */
39	  type: string;
40	  /** Current lifecycle phase (Clerk-owned; spec side of the spec/status split). */
41	  phase: WritPhase;
42	  /**
43	   * Plugin-owned observation slot (status side of the spec/status split).
44	   *
45	   * The observation slot is a plugin-keyed map (`Record<PluginId,
46	   * unknown>`): each top-level key is a plugin id, and the value is an
47	   * arbitrary shape that plugin publishes for post-hoc observation.
48	   * Ownership is convention-only — plugin `X` writes only to `status[X]`.
49	   *
50	   * There is exactly one sanctioned slot-write path:
51	   * `ClerkApi.setWritStatus(writId, pluginId, value)`, which performs a
52	   * transactional read-modify-write on the sub-slot keyed by `pluginId`
53	   * so sibling sub-slots are preserved under concurrent writers.
54	   * `transition()` silently strips `status` from its body, and the
55	   * generic `put()` / `patch()` paths on the writs book are not
56	   * supported slot-write mechanisms — every route other than
57	   * `setWritStatus()` would wholesale-replace the slot and clobber
58	   * sibling sub-slots. Readers access `writ.status?.[pluginId]` directly.
59	   *
60	   * The slot survives terminal phase transitions.
61	   */
62	  status?: Record<string, unknown>;
63	  /** Short human-readable title. */
64	  title: string;
65	  /** Detail text. */
66	  body: string;
67	  /** Target codex name. */
68	  codex?: string;
69	  /** Parent writ id. Absent on root writs. Immutable after creation. */
70	  parentId?: string;
71	  /** ISO timestamp when the writ was created. */
72	  createdAt: string;
73	  /** ISO timestamp of the last mutation. */
74	  updatedAt: string;
75	  /** ISO timestamp when the writ reached a terminal state. */
76	  resolvedAt?: string;
77	  /** Summary of how the writ resolved (set on any terminal transition). */
78	  resolution?: string;
79	}
80	
81	// ── Requests ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
82	
83	/**
84	 * Request to post a new commission (create a writ).
85	 */
86	/**
87	 * Request to edit a writ. Title and body can be edited in any status.
88	 * Type and codex can only be changed while the writ is in 'new' status.
89	 * All fields are optional — only provided fields are updated.
90	 */
91	export interface EditWritRequest {
92	  /** Writ id. */
93	  id: string;
94	  /** New title. */
95	  title?: string;
96	  /** New body text. */
97	  body?: string;
98	  /** New writ type. Must be a valid declared type. */
99	  type?: string;
100	  /** New target codex name. Pass empty string to clear. */

9:- **Observable, reconcilable state.** At any moment, the guild can answer "what is the true current state of session X?" without requiring the component that spawned the session to be running.
35:│ • Reconciler (periodic + startup)                   │
83:│completed ││ failed ││cancelled │  │(reconciled
95:| `failed` | The anima process exited unsuccessfully, or the session host encountered a fatal error, or the reconciler determined the session is no longer alive. | Session host or reconciler |
100:1. **Only one writer at a time, and writers are assigned by state.** A session in `pending` is only written by the guild. A session in `running` is only written by the session host or the reconciler. Terminal states are never written again.
104:3. **`pending` → `running` is host-initiated.** The session host transitions the record to `running` as its first action after it is ready to serve tool calls. This transition carries the information the guild needs to reason about session health (see "Reconciliation").
106:4. **Reconciler may transition `pending` or `running` directly to `failed`.** If the reconciler determines the session host is dead, it writes `failed` with an error describing the reconciliation outcome.
137:The ready report also implicitly refreshes `last_activity_at` (see "Reconciliation").
139:**Heartbeat.** Sent periodically while the session is in `pending` or `running`. Carries only the session identifier. Refreshes `last_activity_at`. See "Reconciliation" for semantics.
182:## Reconciliation
184:Because the session host is detached, the guild cannot directly observe it exiting. The liveness model is **heartbeat-based**: the session host periodically asserts "I am alive," the guild records when it last heard, and a reconciler transitions silent sessions to `failed`.
186:### Heartbeats
188:While a session is in `pending` or `running`, the session host sends an **explicit heartbeat** to the guild on a fixed interval (on the order of 30–60 seconds). The heartbeat is a small HTTP call that updates the session record's `last_activity_at` timestamp.
190:- Heartbeats are **at-least-once delivered** with bounded retry. If the guild is unavailable, the host keeps trying; if the retry budget is exhausted, the heartbeat is dropped (not DLQ'd — a stale heartbeat has no value on replay).
191:- Heartbeats are **independent of tool calls**. Tool-call authorization is a pure read and does not mutate `last_activity_at`. Sessions that are tool-heavy and sessions that are thinking get the same liveness treatment.
192:- The timestamp on the heartbeat is the **guild's wall-clock time at the moment of receipt**, not a host-supplied timestamp. This avoids clock-skew problems between host and guild.
193:- Ready reports and terminal reports implicitly refresh `last_activity_at` too; a host that just reported its state does not need a separate heartbeat in the same instant.
195:### Guild self-heartbeat
197:The guild cannot penalize a session for silence during a window when the guild itself was not listening. To give the reconciler an honest view of host liveness, the guild maintains its own heartbeat: a `guild_alive_at` timestamp in its data store, updated on a timer (same cadence as session heartbeats, or faster).
199:When the guild starts up, it reads the previous `guild_alive_at` from the data store. The gap between that timestamp and `now` is the **downtime window** — the duration for which the guild was not receiving any heartbeats. This window is added as a credit to every non-terminal session's silence budget during the next reconciliation pass.
203:### The reconciler
205:The reconciler scans session records in non-terminal states (`pending` and `running`) and applies a single rule:
207:> **If `now − last_activity_at − downtime_credit > staleness_threshold`, transition the session to `failed`.**
211:- `last_activity_at` is the most recent heartbeat, ready report, or terminal report for that session.
213:- `staleness_threshold` is a fixed value comfortably larger than the heartbeat interval (e.g. 3–5 × heartbeat interval), chosen to tolerate one or two missed heartbeats without declaring the session dead.
215:The reconciler runs:
218:2. **Periodically during guild uptime**, at a cadence comparable to the heartbeat interval. Catches sessions that went silent while the guild was up.
220:A session marked `failed` by the reconciler carries an error describing the reconciliation outcome (for example, "No heartbeat received for 240s — session host presumed dead").
222:### What reconciliation does *not* do
224:- **It does not probe the host's platform directly.** There is no PID check, container runtime query, or network ping. The host's silence is the only signal. This is deliberate: it makes the protocol uniform across local-process hosts, container hosts, and any future host type. A host that cannot heartbeat for 3× the interval is, from the guild's perspective, indistinguishable from a dead host — and that equivalence is the whole point.
225:- **It does not check the anima process directly.** The session host is the supervisor; if the host is heartbeating, the anima process is its problem.
226:- **It does not recover the session.** Reconciliation is a terminal-state transition; it does not attempt to restart anything.
227:- **It does not replace lifecycle reports.** A session host that exits cleanly reports its own terminal state. Reconciliation handles the case where the host cannot report at all.
237:3. If the session host has already died when cancellation is requested, the reconciler catches it on its next scan; the session is marked `failed` rather than `cancelled`, which accurately reflects what happened.
243:- **Local-process host:** the handle is the session host's **process group identifier**. The guild terminates via a signal to the process group (not the process), which guarantees that both the session host *and* the anima process receive the signal even if the host's signal handling is broken. Signalling only the host leaves the anima process orphaned if the host's handler fails to propagate.
271:| Guild restarts during session | Session host continues; tool calls retry with backoff; lifecycle reports DLQ'd if not deliverable; heartbeats are dropped during the outage; on startup the guild computes downtime credit from `guild_alive_at` so sessions are not unfairly reconciled; guild drains DLQ. |
272:| Session host crashes before ready report | Session record stays in `pending`; heartbeats stop; reconciler transitions to `failed` once staleness threshold elapses. |
273:| Session host crashes after ready report, before anima exit | Session record is in `running`; heartbeats stop; reconciler transitions to `failed` once staleness threshold elapses. |
276:| Session host cannot start (bad config, missing dependency) | Host exits non-zero without ever heartbeating; reconciler catches the silent `pending` record once the staleness threshold elapses. |
277:| Cancellation requested while session host is dead | Reconciler catches on next scan; record transitions to `failed`. Cancel request is a no-op. |
289:- Reconciliation is unchanged: a container-hosted session host heartbeats over the same HTTP interface as a local host. If the container dies, heartbeats stop, and the reconciler transitions the session to `failed` on the same rule used for local hosts.
292:The heartbeat-based liveness model is deliberately platform-agnostic: a silent host is a dead host, regardless of whether it was a Unix process or a container. No protocol element changes across host types except the cancellation mechanism.

54:| `'rig-completed'` | Rig reached terminal or stuck state (completed, stuck, failed, or cancelled) |
92:Cancel a running, blocked, or stuck rig. The cancellation cascade:
100:For stuck rigs (engine failure, no active engines), only step 5 applies — the rig transitions directly from `stuck` to `cancelled`.
323:| `rig-cancel` | `write` | Cancel a running, blocked, or stuck rig |
337:| `manual-merge` | quick | Grafted by the `seal` engine — summons the `spider.mender` anima to reconcile rebase conflicts |
345:| `mender` | *(none)* | A merge-conflict reconciliation anima. Summoned by the grafted `manual-merge` engine when Scriptorium reports a rebase conflict during sealing. The role has no tool permissions; it operates through the draft worktree's local `git` binary (available on the host). Its instructions live at `loom-roles/mender.md` in this package. |
351:1. `manual-merge` — summons the `spider.mender` anima in the draft worktree. The anima rebases the draft onto the latest target, resolves conflicts by hand, and emits `### Merge: SUCCESS` when the worktree is ready for a fast-forward push.
352:2. `seal` (retry) — a second `seal` engine with `givens.recover = false`, preventing a second recovery layer.
356:- `recover: false` — the retry seal itself, so a second rebase conflict takes the rig to `stuck`.
359:If the mender emits `### Merge: FAILURE`, emits no marker, or the retry seal also fails, the rig goes `stuck` via the standard `failEngine` path. There is no third attempt.

content.text:
Grounded now. Short version: a lot of what my earlier "MVP shape" proposed already exists, and the click's original framing ("timeouts + reaper") is mostly solved. The real gaps are elsewhere.

## What actually exists in the code today

**Session-level liveness (Animator) — fully built.**
- Babysitter emits `session-heartbeat` every 30s (10s HTTP timeout, best-effort).
- SessionDoc carries `lastActivityAt`; refreshed on ready report, heartbeat, and terminal report. Guild's wall-clock, never host-supplied.
- Reconciler runs every 30s, scans `pending`/`running` sessions, marks `failed` when `now - lastActivityAt - downtimeCredit > 90s`. Error text: `"No heartbeat received for Xs — session host presumed dead (reconciled)"`.
- Guild self-heartbeat (`guild_alive_at`) — on restart, the gap grants downtime credit so sessions aren't unfairly reconciled.
- DLQ at `.nexus/dlq/` — drained *before* reconciler runs (DLQ'd terminals get applied first so the reconciler doesn't clobber real results).
- Cancel path: `cancelHandle` on SessionDoc (tagged: `{ kind: 'local-pgid', pgid }`), SIGTERM propagated to claude, idempotent.
- Legacy records without `lastActivityAt` get backfilled and skipped for one pass.

**Rig/engine cascade — built.**
- Spider's `tryCollect` reads sessions for running engines. On `failed` or `timeout` → `failEngine` → rig `stuck` → CDC cascades writ → `stuck`. On `cancelled` → similar path → writ `cancelled`.
- Explicit comment in spider.ts: *"spider does not reap zombie sessions. Liveness is owned by the animator heartbeat reconciler."* Single path, host-agnostic.
- Writ phases: `new | open | stuck | completed | failed | cancelled`. `stuck` is non-terminal; `stuck → open` is a legal transition (for retry), `stuck → failed/cancelled` also legal.
- Rig statuses: `running | stuck | completed | failed | cancelled | blocked`.

**Concurrency throttle — built.**
- `maxConcurrentEngines` (default 3), `maxConcurrentEnginesPerRig` (default 1). Serial-hopper-MVP = `maxConcurrentEngines: 1`, no code change needed — just config.

## Where the asymmetry is

`SpiderStuckCause` has exactly two values: `'failed-blocker'` and `'cycle'`. Both are gating causes, written to `status.spider.stuckCause` when Spider stucks a writ via the *gating* path. The **engine-cascade stuck path does not write that slot**.

Consequence: `autoUnstick` uses slot presence as the load-bearing signal for "this is mine, I can recover it." Which means every engine-cascade stuck writ — the common case, the one that actually happens when a session dies — is:

- Structurally opaque (no machine-readable "why")
- Excluded from auto-unstick (by design, because auto-unstick doesn't know how to recover engine failures)
- Only describable via the free-text `resolution` field

That's fine as a MVP state — it means engine-cascade stucks are firmly "human decides" until retry policy exists. But it's the gap that matters.

## What's genuinely missing

1. **Retry primitive.** Does not exist at rig/writ level. The only "retry" today is:
   - `SealYields.retries` (seal-internal, unrelated to rig retries)
   - A `retries` link *label* the UI sets when the operator manually reposts a stuck writ (documentary, not load-bearing)
   - Seal-engine's manual-merge + one-retry-seal (seal-specific)

   No attempt counter on rigs or writs. No `rig-retry` command. No "stuck → open" operator path surfaced as a CLI. No clockwork to automate it.

2. **Structured stuck-cause for engine-cascade stucks.** As above. Prereq for any retry policy — otherwise policy has to keyword-match free-text resolutions.

3. **Rig wall-clock bound.** A session can heartbeat happily for hours. Heartbeat catches hangs, not ambitious-wrong-path runs. Probably *not* MVP-critical — the serial hopper can afford one long session; cost is wall-clock, not correctness. But worth naming.

4. **Operator-facing stuck surfaces.** `rig-list` and `writ-list` filter by status; no dedicated "what's stuck and why" view; no `writ-requeue` or `rig-retry` command; no per-stuck-cause filter.

## Updated MVP framing

The click's stated shape — "stuck detection (timeouts + reaper)" — is a solved problem. The real MVP work sits under the same click but is differently shaped:

### MVP slice A: stuck taxonomy (small, foundational, ship first)

- Extend `SpiderStuckCause` to cover engine-cascade causes. Candidate values:
  - `'session-failed'` — session reconciled or exited non-zero
  - `'session-cancelled'` — someone cancelled the session mid-flight
  - `'engine-error'` — exception thrown in engine run/collect
  - `'graft-invalid'` — graft validation failed
  - `'unknown-design'` — engine design not found
  - `'unknown-block-type'` — block type not registered
  - (keep existing `'failed-blocker'`, `'cycle'`)
- Write `status.spider.stuckCause` at every existing `failEngine` call site (all the `rig-completed, outcome: 'stuck'` returns in spider.ts). They currently have the error message but not the cause tag.
- Decision: does `autoUnstick` start ignoring or recognizing the new causes? Default: **ignore** — MVP keeps engine-cascade stucks human-triaged, same as today. The slot is there for observability and future retry policy, not to widen auto-unstick's scope.

**Size:** small. A type extension, a handful of call-site updates, one migration concern for already-stuck writs (backfill via `resolution` text pattern or leave them absent — probably leave them absent; they're already triaged manually).

### MVP slice B: the retry primitive (bulk of the work)

- Add attempt counter — `status.spider.attempts` on the writ. Increment on rig spawn.
- Add `rig-retry` (or `writ-requeue`) operator command: `stuck → open`, Spider's `trySpawn` picks it up on next crawl, attempts counter drives bound. Semantics-wise this is "new rig on same writ," consistent with writ=obligation/rig=attempt.
- Decide attempt bound. Default: **N=2 attempts total** for anything in `'session-failed'` / `'engine-error'` buckets; **N=1 (no auto-retry)** for `'graft-invalid'` / `'unknown-design'` / `'unknown-block-type'` (these are definitional bugs, retry won't help).
- Automated retry mechanism: a simple clockwork/standing-order that scans stuck writs where `stuckCause ∈ {retryable set}` and `attempts < bound`, and performs the requeue. This is the "hopper keeps running on flaky failures" behavior.
- Question (small): does attempts reset on operator-initiated retry? **Yes** — operator intervention is the escape hatch.

### Not MVP

- Wall-clock rig timeouts
- Conditional retry by exponential backoff
- Retry-starvation fairness (degenerate at serial = 1)
- Auto-retry of gating-caused stucks (they already auto-unstick via `autoUnstick`)
- Stuck-cause enum for writ types beyond Spider's (Clerk-level cause taxonomy) — separate concern

## The refined recommendation

Pick up Slice A first — structured stuck taxonomy. It's small, it's substrate, and it unblocks everything else cleanly. Then Slice B — the retry primitive — which is the actual "unstuck the hopper" mechanism the click is really about.

**One question for you:** on Slice A, the migration decision for already-stuck writs (backfill vs leave absent). I lean leave-absent because the slot is newly consequential and older stucks are already resolved by human attention. But if you'd rather backfill (best-guess from `resolution` text pattern match), that's a tiny addition. Which way?

content.text:
Verified both assumptions — one confirms your suspicion, one you may not have noticed.

## Ground truth, verified

**1. `autoUnstick` does NOT follow `retries` links.**

It walks the `blockerIds` the dependent was stuck on *originally*. For `failed-blocker`, it requires every one of those *original* blockers to reach `completed` or `cancelled`. It never looks at any other writ.

Consequence for the current repost flow, today: if B is blocking A via `spider.follows`, B stucks, B gets reposted as B', and B' completes — **A stays stuck forever**. B is still in `failed`, so autoUnstick's check fails. The `retries` link is completely invisible to the gating machinery.

This is already a bug in the reposting model for any gated chain. The machinery is 1:1 between "declared blocker" and "gate release." The only way out today is to manually rewrite A's `spider.follows` edge from B to B', or cancel A and repost it with new links. I don't think we've actually hit this because most commissions in flight haven't had load-bearing gated chains — but spider.follows just shipped, so we will.

**2. `trySpawn` strictly enforces 1:1 rig-per-writ.**

```ts
const existing = await rigsBook.find({ where: [['writId', '=', writ.id]], limit: 1 });
if (existing.length > 0) continue;
```

That's the *only* enforcement of the 1:1 invariant in the whole Spider. Everywhere else (CDC, cascades, list queries) already handles N gracefully — the cascade queries `rigsBook.find({ writId })` and handles whatever it finds.

So "multi-rig on writ" isn't a substrate change. It's a policy change in one spot.

## The three actual options

### Option 1 — Keep reposting, make gates follow retries

Work needed:
- Extend `autoUnstick` to follow `retries` links: "blocker B is in failed, but B is retries-linked by B' which is in completed → treat as released"
- *Or* auto-relink inbound `spider.follows` on repost (mutates author edges silently)
- Retry attempt count = walk `retries` chain backward

Pros: no rig-model change; patron brief can mutate between attempts.
Cons: either makes `retries` a load-bearing link type (semantics change from documentary to structural), or does silent edge rewriting; `retries` is also currently a free-form *label*, not a `kind`, so we'd be promoting a free-text label to load-bearing substrate. That contradicts the work we just shipped that moved load-bearing semantics onto `semanticMeaning`/`kind` deliberately.

### Option 2 — Multi-rig-lite

Work needed:
- Change the trySpawn guard from "writ has no rig" to "writ has no *active* rig" (active = running/blocked; stuck/failed/cancelled/completed are eligible for a new attempt)
- Add a `writ-requeue` (or `rig-retry`) command: transitions stuck → open, Spider picks up via trySpawn
- Decide old-stuck-rig disposition: leave as stuck historical record (simplest), OR add `'superseded'` rig status when a fresh attempt starts
- Retry attempt count = `rigs.length` for the writ (derived)
- `rig-for-writ` semantics: return latest rig (default), list-all variant for history

Pros: aligns with the designed writ=obligation / rig=attempt model; spider.follows edges stay stable (point at the writ, which is the obligation); autoUnstick needs zero change because the writ completing releases the gate; gate semantics work for free.
Cons: some observability work (rig-list and UI will show N rigs per writ); new operator command; the "which rig is current" semantics need a crisp answer.

### Option 3 — Hybrid: multi-rig-lite for automated retry, repost for patron-initiated retry

They're actually semantically different:
- **Automated retry**: "same obligation, new attempt" → N rigs on same writ. What the hopper does when a rig stucks from flaky causes.
- **Patron manual retry**: "new obligation, informed by the previous failure, possibly with brief changes" → new writ with `retries` label. What the patron does when they've looked at a failure and want to tweak and try again.

Pros: each mechanism matches its use case; no forcing one model to cover both; the repost flow stays as-is (it already works for patron-initiated retries on simple cases), and we build multi-rig-lite for automation.
Cons: two mechanisms to reason about — though in practice the patron doesn't care about rig internals and the hopper doesn't touch the repost flow.

## Autonomy of each option on the broader multi-rig tree

- Option 1 doesn't touch the rig model — but it loads the `retries` link type with structural meaning we'd have to keep alive when the broader multi-rig refactor lands.
- Option 2 *is* starting the multi-rig refactor, but with a minimal slice. It doesn't commit us to rig-intent vocabulary, Fabricator rig-composition, typed artifacts, or brief/mandate collapse — those are all orthogonal.
- Option 3 is Option 2 + leaves repost alone. Cheapest total delta.

## My recommendation

**Option 3.** Multi-rig-lite for the automation path, keep reposting unchanged for patron-initiated manual retries. Specifically:

- The trySpawn guard change is one-line
- `writ-requeue` is a new operator command (small)
- Old stuck rig disposition: **leave as stuck** (don't introduce `'superseded'` yet — it's premature; the "latest" rig for a writ is determinable by createdAt ordering, and historical stuck rigs show retry attempts naturally)
- Attempt counter: derived, `rigs-for-writ.length`, no persistence
- On the gating side: **no change needed** — gates point at writ, writ completes, gate releases
- Patron manual retry via repost: unchanged

And **the gated-chain bug needs calling out** regardless of which option you pick — because once `spider.follows` is in active use, any reposted writ that had inbound blockers leaves the dependents stranded. That's a silent correctness issue. Multi-rig-lite fixes it by construction; repost-plus-autoUnstick-walks-retries fixes it by policy; but we can't just leave it.

**Question:** does Option 3's split (two mechanisms, different use cases) match your intuition, or do you want them collapsed into one? The collapse-into-one argument is "simpler mental model"; the split argument is "they're actually different actions with different semantics."

Write `status.spider.stuckCause` at every `failEngine` call site. Decision: keep `autoUnstick` ignoring new causes at MVP (slot is for observability + future retry policy, not wider auto-recovery). Migration of already-stuck writs: leave absent (don't backfill). Likely parent: c-mo28k7ri (stuck-rig detection and recovery).
   - **Click D — Slice B (retry primitive)**: Attempt counter (derived as `rigs.length` for writ, not persisted), `writ-requeue` (or `rig-retry`) operator command transitioning stuck → open, clockwork automation for retryable causes, attempt bounds (N=2 for `session-failed`/`engine-error`; N=1 for definitional bugs). Likely parent: c-mo28k8ir (failure and retry policy).

   Additional open design questions to capture as sub-clicks or notes:
   - Old-stuck-rig disposition when new attempt spawns: leave as stuck vs add `'superseded'` status (Coco lean: leave as stuck).
   - `rig-for-writ` semantics under N-rigs-per-writ: latest by default, list-all variant.
   - Does operator-initiated retry reset the attempts counter? (Coco lean: yes — operator intervention is the escape hatch.)

8. Current Work:
   Immediately before the summary request, Sean said:
   
   > "create click for gated-chain bug, and which option to follow here. also, create/update clicks based on the earlier path a/path b fork if needed"
   
   This followed my analysis that verified autoUnstick does NOT follow retries links and that trySpawn's 1:1 rig-per-writ enforcement is a one-line check. I had presented three retry-mechanism options and recommended Option 3 (hybrid: multi-rig-lite for automated retry + keep reposting for patron manual retry). I also flagged the gated-chain bug as a silent correctness issue requiring its own click. The Slice A (stuck taxonomy) / Slice B (retry primitive) fork from my earlier message also needs to be captured in the click tree.
   
   I had NOT yet created or updated any clicks. The next session needs to execute the click creation.

9. Optional Next Step:
   Create the clicks using `nsg click new` commands. Sean's direct quote from his most recent message:
   
   > "create click for gated-chain bug, and which option to follow here. also, create/update clicks based on the earlier path a/path b fork if needed"
   
   Concrete click-creation plan (parents inferred from conversation context):
   
   1. Create **gated-chain bug** click under c-mo2z00fa (retry ergonomics for load-bearing links) — goal: "Reposted writs leave dependents stranded — autoUnstick walks original blockerIds, not retries links, so spider.follows gates never release after repost-based recovery". Body should note this is a silent correctness issue now that spider.follows is shipping; multi-rig-lite would fix by construction.
   
   2. Create **retry-mechanism option choice** click under c-mo28k8ir (failure and retry policy) or c-mo28k3ar — goal: "Choose between repost+autoUnstick-extension, multi-rig-lite, or hybrid for automated retry". Enumerate Options 1/2/3 with tradeoffs; note Coco recommendation is Option 3 (hybrid).
   
   3. Create **Slice A — structured stuck taxonomy** click under c-mo28k7ri — goal: "Extend SpiderStuckCause enum to cover engine-cascade causes and write status.spider.stuckCause at all failEngine call sites".
   
   4. Create **Slice B — retry primitive** click under c-mo28k8ir — goal: "Attempt counter, writ-requeue command, and clockwork automation for stuck writs with retryable causes".
   
   Before executing, I should confirm with Sean whether these parent assignments match his intent, since the Slice A/B framing emerged from my analysis and he may want to adjust placement, or whether he wants a fresh top-level click for the option-choice question vs parenting under an existing serial-hopper-milestone child.

If you need specific details from before compaction (like exact code snippets, error messages, or content you generated), read the full transcript at: /home/vscode/.claude/projects/-workspace-nexus-mk2/8f67ada8-9024-4407-b4b3-b4cbf0a32fca.jsonl
Continue the conversation from where it left off without asking the user any further questions. Resume directly — do not acknowledge the summary, do not recap what was happening, do not preface with "I'll continue" or similar. Pick up the last task as if the break never happened.

file.content:
# Nexus Mk 2.1 — Sanctum

This is the sanctum: the patron-side home base for the Nexus Mk 2.1 project. It holds experiments, research, operational tooling, and the domain model — but not the framework source code. The framework lives at `/workspace/nexus/`.

Nexus Mk 2.1 is also a documented experiment — Sean is exploring AI-enabled development practices and intends to publish findings.

## Project Structure

```
nexus-mk2/  (the sanctum)
├── experiments/                 # Research & experiment tracking
│   ├── X001–X012/              #   Numbered experiments, each with spec.md + artifacts/
│   └── ethnography/            #   Qualitative research on human-agent interaction
│       ├── interviews/          #   Ethnographer interview transcripts (timestamped)
│       ├── session-notes/       #   Per-session observations (new/ → reviewed/ pipeline)
│       └── transcripts/         #   Design session transcripts, interview source material
├── domain/                      # Separate git repo, mounted read-only for agents
│   ├── ontology/                #   TypeScript type definitions — formal domain model
│   ├── requirements/            #   System requirements (YAML)
│   └── backlog/                 #   Feature ideas not yet commissioned
├── docs/                        # Sanctum-side documentation
│   ├── future/                  #   Backlog: known-gaps, uncommissioned feature ideas
│   └── archive/                 #   Completed commissions, deprecated docs
├── bin/                         # Shell scripts for operations
│   ├── coco.sh                  #   Launch Coco session
│   ├── commission.sh            #   Post a commission to the Clerk (Spider dispatches)
│   └── ...                      #   Other operational scripts (some legacy Mk 2.0)
├── .claude/                     # Claude Code agent configuration
│   ├── CLAUDE.md                #   THIS FILE — shared instructions for all agents
│   ├── agents/                  #   Agent-specific instructions (coco.md, ethnographer.md)
│   ├── skills/                  #   Reusable skill definitions (wrap-up/)
│   └── worktrees/               #   Snapshot of old worktree state (historical)
├── .artifacts/                  # Build & assessment artifacts (JSON, timestamped)
├── .scratch/                    # Ephemeral working space
│   ├── recent-sessions/         #   Coco session summaries (read at startup for continuity)
│   └── todo/                    #   Tabled items: parked specs/analysis to pick up later
└── .locks/                      # Feature locks for concurrent agent coordination
```

## Related Repositories

- **`/workspace/nexus/`** — the framework source code (TypeScript packages, published to npm)
- **`/workspace/vibers/`** — the live guild workspace where animas operate

## Key Concepts

- **Guild** — an instantiated workspace where animas operate. Created via `nsg init`.
- **Anima** — an AI identity with a name, role, curriculum, and temperament.
- **Commission** — a posted unit of work from the patron. Creates a `mandate` writ.
- **Writ** — a typed, tree-structured record kept in the guild's books with a lifecycle (`new → open → stuck → completed/failed/cancelled`). The type names the kind of record: `mandate` for an obligation. Dispatch to a rig is opt-in per writ type.
- **Click** — an atomic decision-node managed by the Ratchet apparatus. Tracks questions and inquiries (the reasoning domain), organized in a tree. Lifecycle: `live → parked → concluded/dropped`. Clicks are the session-continuity mechanism for interactive work; writs track obligations.
- **Clockworks** — the event-driven dispatch layer. Standing orders bind event patterns to handlers (engines or summons).
- **Session** — a single agent invocation through the session funnel (manifest → launch → record).
- **Conversation** — multi-turn interaction grouping multiple sessions via `--resume`.

## Foundational Documents

- **[Project Philosophy](/workspace/nexus/docs/philosophy.md)** — the "why" of this project: experiment goals, Mk 2.0 vs 2.1 differences, the human-as-user model.
- **[Guild Metaphor](/workspace/nexus/docs/guild-metaphor.md)** — the conceptual model and vocabulary for the system. Read this to understand guild terminology (anima, commission, writ, clockworks, etc.) and write in the correct register.

## Directives

- **Self-document for other agents.** Write commit messages, code comments, and documentation with the assumption that your primary audience is other agents who will continue the work. Be precise and concise; include enough context for an agent to pick up where you left off.
- **Respect the human boundary.** Certain agents (like Coco) are interactive and have their own interaction rules. All other agents are autonomous and should minimize unnecessary human interaction.
- **Commit early and often.** Make small, atomic commits as work is completed. Do not accumulate large uncommitted changesets. Never leave uncommitted or untracked files in the project root. This is critical in a multi-agent environment where conflicts are a real risk.
- **Minimize conflict surface.** Structure work to reduce the likelihood of git conflicts with other agents. Prefer adding new files over modifying shared ones. When modifying shared files, keep changes narrow and well-scoped. Commit and merge promptly rather than holding long-lived branches.
- **Date-prefix experiment artifacts.** Files in `experiments/X*/artifacts/` use the naming convention `YYYY-MM-DD-<slug>.ext` (e.g., `2026-04-03-findings.md`). Use the date the artifact was created. This keeps artifacts chronologically sortable within each experiment.

content.text:
Four clicks on the board:

- **Gated-chain bug** — parked under retry-ergonomics-for-load-bearing-links. Captures the silent correctness issue: `autoUnstick` walks original `blockerIds` and doesn't follow `retries`, so reposted writs strand dependents once `spider.follows` is shipping.
- **Retry mechanism option choice** — under failure-and-retry-policy. Options 1/2/3 enumerated in the goal. My recommendation stays Option 3 (hybrid).
- **Slice A — structured stuck taxonomy** — under stuck-rig-detection. Observability-only at MVP, no `autoUnstick` widening.
- **Slice B — retry primitive** — under failure-and-retry-policy. Linked `depends-on` Slice A and `depends-on` the option-choice click.

Also linked the gated-chain bug `related` to the option-choice click — Options 2 and 3 would fix it by construction, so the decision outcome determines whether gated-chain needs its own fix or gets swept along.

**Natural next move:** resolve the option-choice click. Once that's locked in, Slice A can start in parallel (it's independent of the mechanism choice), and Slice B picks up whichever retry shape the decision selects. Want to work through the option choice now, or break for the evening?

message.content: whats the options choice click id?

content: Goal is unlocking autonomous hopper operation; we just spun up four clicks covering the gated-chain bug, retry-mechanism option choice, stuck-taxonomy slice, and retry-primitive slice. Next action is resolving the option choice (c-mo56pq2k) between repost-extension, multi-rig-lite, or hybrid.

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /workspace/nexus-mk2/.claude/skills/wrap-up

# Wrap-Up: Complete Work and Generate Session Summary

## Generate Summary

Generate a structured summary of the current session. This summary serves one audience:

**The Scribe** — as input for session notes and published material.

Session-to-session continuity is now carried by **clicks** (see Coco's agent file and the clicks skill). Before generating the wrap-up summary, conclude or park any clicks touched this session — the click tree is what the next Coco session will read for orientation, not this wrap-up file.

<expected-format>
# Session Summary

## What we did

- Bulleted list of concrete actions taken (files created/modified, decisions made, commits pushed). Reference specific files and commit hashes.

## Decisions made and/or deferred

- What was decided, including explicit "we chose NOT to" decisions. Brief rationale for each.

## Next steps & open questions

- Concrete actions for the next session or for agents to pick up.
- Open questions which were raised but not answered

### Notable moments

Capture quotes, insights, or exchanges that stand out — either for system design or for the research/experiment narrative.

For each notable moment:
- Include **direct quotes from Sean** when available
- Flag **why it's notable** (e.g., "shifted the project's core abstraction", "surfaced a tension between X and Y", "revealed an assumption about how agents should work")
- Prioritize moments where the human changed direction, challenged an assumption, or articulated something new about the project's purpose

### Experiment observations

Flag anything relevant to active experiments. Omit this section if nothing applies.

<expected-format>

## Complete Work

After wrapping up, **ALWAYS**:

- Commit any changes you have made, if any
- Push main to the remote

## Reminders

Check for things Sean should be nudged about:

- **Ethnographer interview:** Check the most recent interview file in `experiments/ethnography/interviews/` (files named `YYYY-MM-DDTHHMMSS.md`). If the most recent one is more than a day old — or if none exist yet — remind Sean to run an ethnographer session. Keep it light: *"It's been a couple days since your last ethnographer interview — might be a good time to check in."*

## Guidelines

- Keep the whole summary under ~500 words. Brevity is a feature.
- Do not editorialize beyond the "why it's notable" flags.
- Write the summary to `experiments/ethnography/session-notes/new/<timestamp>.md`. where `<timestamp>` is the current date and time formatted as `YYYY-MM-DDTHHMMSS.md` (use the actual current time — don't round to the nearest hour). Each session gets its own file — never overwrite a previous one. This file is for the ethnographer and must NOT be auto-deleted — it accumulates until the ethnographer reviews it.
- Commit all `session-notes`, and `experiments/data/transcripts/` changes to git and push those changes. The transcripts directory may contain files from concurrent sessions — commit whatever is there.
- In addition to writing the summary, share it in the chat for review and inclusion in session transcripts

input.content:
# Session Summary

Session: 8f67ada8-9024-4407-b4b3-b4cbf0a32fca

## What we did

- Oriented on the autonomous-operation click (c-mo1mq8ry) and picked up the stuck-rig detection line under the serial-hopper milestone.
- Did an exhaustive scan through the nexus code to ground stuck-detection framing in the actual implementation: `animator/src/startup.ts` (reconciler + 90s staleness threshold), `animator/src/animator.ts` (30s heartbeat interval), `claude-code/src/babysitter.ts` (DLQ + heartbeat), `spider/src/spider.ts` (failEngine, tryCollect, autoUnstick, trySpawn), `spider/src/types.ts` (SpiderStuckCause enum), `clerk/src/types.ts` (writ phase state machine).
- Reframed MVP for stuck-rig detection: session-level timeout/reaping is already solved by the animator heartbeat reconciler. The real gap is structured stuck-cause taxonomy + a retry primitive — not "build detection from scratch."
- Examined the retry question: current workflow reposts writs with a free-form `retries` link; writ state machine never transitions back out of stuck. Verified by reading spider.ts that `autoUnstick` walks original `blockerIds` and does NOT follow retries links — confirms the gated-chain stranding bug.
- Located the 1:1 rig-per-writ invariant — a single `if (existing.length > 0) continue` at spider.ts:2091 — making multi-rig-lite a small surgical change.
- Enumerated three retry-mechanism options: (1) repost+autoUnstick-extension, (2) multi-rig-lite, (3) hybrid. Recommended hybrid.
- Captured four clicks: gated-chain bug, retry-mechanism option choice, Slice A (stuck taxonomy), Slice B (retry primitive). Linked Slice B `depends-on` both Slice A and the option choice; linked gated-chain bug `related` to option choice (Options 2/3 fix it by construction).

## Decisions made and/or deferred

- **Deferred:** retry-mechanism option choice (captured as its own click). Coco recommends hybrid.
- **Decided:** Slice A keeps `autoUnstick` ignoring new stuck causes at MVP — the enum widening is for observability and future retry policy, not wider auto-recovery.
- **Decided:** manual patron retry (new brief, repost flow) and automated retry (same obligation, new attempt) are semantically distinct and do not need to share a mechanism.
- **Deferred:** c-mo3d6lyd (evidence-based decisional machinery) — acknowledged as MVP-adjacent for decision quality, but Patron Anima work (in flight as w-mo4bke5x) covers enough of the near-term need.

## Next steps & open questions

- Resolve the retry-mechanism option choice (c-mo56pq2k). Once locked, Slice A can start in parallel; Slice B shape follows the decision.
- Open: old-stuck-rig disposition when a new attempt spawns under multi-rig-lite — leave as stuck, or add a `'superseded'` status? Coco leans leave-as-stuck.
- Open: does operator-initiated retry reset the attempts counter? Coco leans yes — operator intervention is the escape hatch.
- Open: `rig-for-writ` semantics under N-rigs-per-writ — latest by default, list-all variant?

### Notable moments

- **Sean:** *"our current retry workflow is... rigs stay stuck, writs never leave the failed/stuck state. Instead, the writ is reposted with a 'retries' link."* — surfaced that the current retry mechanism is documentary metadata grafted on top of an unchanged state machine, not a real retry primitive. Shifted the framing from "add timeouts and a reaper" to "build a real retry primitive on top of existing heartbeat-based stuck detection."
- **Coco correction moment:** initial stuck-detection framing assumed nothing existed. Sean's instruction to do an exhaustive code scan revealed most of the detection mechanism is already built (heartbeat reconciler + staleness threshold) — the MVP work is structured stuck-cause taxonomy and a retry primitive, not detection from scratch. Notable as an instance of jumping to design before grounding in implementation.
- **Coco correction moment 2:** initial MVP framing treated the serial-hopper milestone (c-mo28k3ar) as the whole autonomous-operation MVP. Sean probed with c-mo3d6lyd (decisional machinery), forcing a reframe to two orthogonal axes — execution mechanics and decision quality — with Patron Anima covering the decision-quality axis for MVP.

### Experiment observations

- X008 (Criteria-Internalization Path): the commission-review pattern is still patron-initiated; no new signal this session.

    date: 2026-04-18
    item: "Continued Patron Anima MVP scoping under c-mo3o0euk — taste corpus, family-vs-single, and a clicks-skill instruction update. Opened with c-mo3o2a8h (taste corpus). Did inventory of 13+ taste-signal sources with per-source signal/volume/cost ratings; initially proposed a three-layer raw/index/digest corpus architecture with runtime retrieval. Sean reframed entirely: Patron Anima is guild-specific, not framework; corpus is packaged as role instructions = system prompt prose on a 'patron' role defined in the guild and installed via Astrolabe config. This collapsed corpus from a runtime retrieval pipeline to a docs-authoring pipeline — big simplification. Concluded c-mo3o2a8h with that framing. Spawned + concluded c-mo3tivzf (skip patron steps when no role configured; no framework default — patrons opt in by authoring; a 'default patron' is a contradiction because every patron has different taste by definition). Opened live c-mo3tiwy6 as future-state: role instructions produced by function() rather than static .md pointer, which would unlock per-session anima construction. Per Sean's follow-up, decomposed the taste-corpus inventory into 14 child clicks under c-mo3o2a8h, each concluded as include/don't-include with curation particulars: 7 include (concluded click conclusions, patron feedback on plans, commission review comments, session notes, philosophy/guild-metaphor docs, CLAUDE files, ethnographer interviews); 7 don't-include (design session transcripts, Coco chat transcripts, commission log, Coco log, click tree shape, git history, briefs-drafted-vs-posted). One click mis-goaled and dropped/recreated (c-mo3tpwxc → c-mo3tqf56) after I conflated interviews with session notes. Moved to c-mo3o2b49 (one-vs-family): concluded single-patron-for-MVP with named forcing functions for an eventual split — Sean's taste across domains is largely unified principles applied to different surfaces, principles-list format captures this, per-domain diagnostic slicing achievable via decision-level domain tags rather than patron fanout. Parked c-mo3txast (design the domain-tag schema) as a related follow-up. Correction earlier in session: I initially claimed the session-notes new/→reviewed/ directory split was a validation/provisionality pipeline — Sean corrected that it's just ethnographer bookkeeping (has-been-interviewed flag), no epistemic weight. Good catch on confabulation-from-directory-structure. Meta-thread: Sean asked whether the sub-click decomposition of the inventory could have been automatic without prompting. Diagnosed honestly — I was self-censoring on tree-clutter grounds against coco.md's 'capture liberally' guidance, and the clicks skill lacked a concrete drafting-time trigger for enumerated-analysis-with-per-item-decisions. Added a new 'Enumerated decisions → enumerated children' subsection to .claude/skills/clicks/SKILL.md with a specific recognition trigger ('if you catch yourself writing a conclusion that contains a per-item list with individual rationale, stop — that list is telling you the decisions want their own clicks'). Commit covers the skill edit + this log entry."

Sean then reframed these cleanly: ask-patron is always provided, may return 'patron unavailable' sentinel, Distiller degrades to best-effort — unifies MVP and guilds-without-Patron as the same permanent code path. Interview transcript reachable via PlanDoc → session link → transcript (no copy); not surfaced as a Sage-facing working source for now (audit-only); reversible. **Major terminology shift Sean drove**: the patron's submitted input artifact is now called petition (formerly brief); the Distiller's structured output is now called brief (was being called spec/clarified-feature-spec); the implementer-facing artifact stays as spec. Pipeline reads petition → [Distiller] → brief → [Sage] → spec → [implementer] → code. The downstream worker name 'analyst / reader-analyst' becomes Sage. Self-uncertainty resolved along two surfaces: verdict-time defaults to confirm with low confidence (no abstain, no fallback meta-heuristics — meta-preferences belong in the principles list as high-confidence principles); interview-time may abstain with 'no signal' (asymmetry justified by larger blast radius of confident-wrong at interview-time). Principle-grounded confidence calibration (1 principle → high, conflict → med, none → low) surfaced as an authoring hint. Sean pushed back productively twice: first when I objected that commissioning Patron Anima was premature because host machinery (Distiller, analyst, decision-review) didn't exist — he correctly pointed out the anima's interface is a clean contract that can be declared and built against, no prerequisite dependency. Second when I proposed splitting into two briefs (role machinery + engine), Sean directed we collapse to one: patron config belongs as an Astrolabe config variable (vars.astrolabe.patronRole) consumed by the engine via template substitution, engine no-ops when unset, per-decision patron section parallel to existing recommendation/rationale on PlanDoc.Decision. On failure policy Sean picked single-pass fall-through over bounded retries ('stuck-but-recoverable-with-answers > stuck-and-requires-repost'); on session shape, single-session for MVP with per-decision sessions captured as post-MVP option (c-mo4bi1gy). New clicks opened during scoping: c-mo49zsst (planner meta-heuristics for low-confidence as a separate planner-strategy question, parented under decisional-machinery grandparent), c-mo4bi1gy (per-decision session alternative, post-MVP bucket), c-mo4bla71 (reporting surface for override-rate × confidence data, post-MVP bucket), c-mo4blbm3 (author Sean's patron-anima role file — MVP-adjacent execution work, top-level). Brief-authoring journey itself produced useful reshaping: initial draft assumed a framework-level 'patron' concept and two commissions; verifying the Astrolabe rig template (plan-and-ship.ts) and decision-review engine grounded the real integration point between inventory-check and decision-review, which paralleled the existing 'analyst pre-decides → decision-review auto-skips' pattern exactly. Meta-observation worth surfacing: I twice produced a structural objection (premature-commissioning, too-lean-brief) that dissolved under Sean's pushback — a pattern of false-alarm from pattern-matching rather than checking the actual interface contract. Worth keeping an eye on in future design sessions."
    commissionable: false
    justification: sanctum

    date: 2026-04-18
    item: "Continued Distiller subtree under c-mo3qj676. Concluded c-mo3qjbmw (BA process) with Shape C — plan-then-interview phasing: one LLM turn produces extract+extrapolate draft + structured gap list, then a 0–N turn interview loop closes gaps via batched ask-patron, then emit. Decision criteria table for extract/extrapolate/interview/open-question with provenance tags. Open Questions are a universal unresolved bucket regardless of why (declined / budget / no-patron / no-anchor / triaged-out) — Sean's ask, intended to leave room for downstream handler stages. Loosened extrapolation to also accept named methodological-principle anchors (closed enumerated set in role instructions, e.g. minimal-coherence, symmetric-transitions); spawned c-mo4a0s3x to design the principle set itself. Sean pushed back hard on whether sealing the Distiller against fabrication just relocates the problem — agreed, Distiller stays sealed but the system needs an explicit Open-Questions handler. Spawned c-mo49uxov as new sibling under BA/PO parent with four enumerated child options (a Patron-Anima second pass, b Bold Agent / boldness budget, c threshold-to-human, d committee-style techniques) plus c-mo4anoev to update Sage instructions for the eventual handler outputs. Concluded c-mo3qjcuw (interview mechanics) — hard cap of 3 turns with quiescence early termination, telemetry meta block on the spec recording interview_turns/terminated_by/questions_per_turn, batched questions grouped by topic, three question shapes (open/closed/multiple-choice) with bias toward MC when options extractable, non-leading constraint, target field on every question, spillover integration when patron answers address slots beyond the question target, three-layer defense for product-layer-only (Distiller instructions + Patron refusal c-mo49h2a5 + audit on emit). Spawned c-mo49h2a5 under MVP Patron Anima scope for the patron-side refusal rule. Concluded c-mo3qsanr (Sage input contract) with Position 2 — brief is primary, petition is attached secondary, Sage may consult petition when addressing Open Questions, marked blanks, missed-gap unknowns (Sean's ask — Distiller is fallible and sealing the petition off would force silent invention), or brief-internal contradictions; may NOT use petition to override brief content the Distiller already filled (that's a Distiller defect to surface upstream). Interview transcript reachable via PlanDoc → session link → transcript (no copy); not surfaced as a Sage-facing working source for now (audit-only); reversible. **Major terminology shift Sean drove**: the patron's submitted input artifact is now called petition (formerly brief); the Distiller's structured output is now called brief (was being called spec/clarified-feature-spec); the implementer-facing artifact stays as spec. Pipeline reads petition → [Distiller] → brief → [Sage] → spec → [implementer] → code. The downstream worker name 'analyst / reader-analyst' becomes Sage. Vocabulary tome updated: Distiller entry rewritten with new terminology + cross-refs to c-mo3qjbmw and c-mo3qjcuw, Sage entry added, Petition entry generalized from 'internal commission' to 'any formally submitted request' (patron-source vs internal-source as a source field, not separate types) with the existing internal-commission bookmark reframed as the internal-source variant, Brief entry added with the historical-mapping note. Spawned c-mo4bkd63 under c-mo3qj676 to track the actual migration cleanup (briefs skill rename, bin/commission.sh, Coco's CLAUDE.md, doc sweeps); explicitly out of scope: rewriting historical click conclusions (write-once) and renaming Sage in code (no Sage code exists yet). Three Distiller-subtree clicks now closed; c-mo3s410k (edge-case classification criteria) remains live as the only loose end under the parent."

    justification: sanctum

Meta-observation worth surfacing: I twice produced a structural objection (premature-commissioning, too-lean-brief) that dissolved under Sean's pushback — a pattern of false-alarm from pattern-matching rather than checking the actual interface contract. Worth keeping an eye on in future design sessions."
    commissionable: false
    justification: sanctum

  - session: 8f67ada8-9024-4407-b4b3-b4cbf0a32fca
    date: 2026-04-19
    item: "Design session on autonomous-operation MVP — stuck-rig detection and retry mechanics. Reframed stuck detection after exhaustive nexus code scan: session-level timeout + reaping is already solved by the animator heartbeat reconciler (30s heartbeat, 90s staleness, DLQ on unreachable guild). The real MVP gap is structured stuck-cause taxonomy + a retry primitive, not detection-from-scratch. Walked the retry workflow with Sean: current approach reposts writs with a free-form retries link, writs never transition back out of stuck, and autoUnstick (verified by reading spider.ts:2008-2062) walks original blockerIds and does NOT follow retries links — confirming the gated-chain stranding bug that becomes live once spider.follows ships. Also located the 1:1 rig-per-writ invariant as a single one-line check at spider.ts:2091 (const existing = await rigsBook.find...if (existing.length > 0) continue), making multi-rig-lite a small surgical change. Enumerated three retry-mechanism options — (1) repost + extend autoUnstick to follow retries, (2) multi-rig-lite relaxing 1:1 with attempts = rigs.length, (3) hybrid using multi-rig-lite for automated retry and keeping reposting for patron-initiated manual retry with brief changes. Recommended Option 3: manual patron retry (new brief, repost) and automated retry (same obligation, new attempt) are semantically distinct and don't benefit from a unified mechanism. Captured four new clicks: c-mo56pnqd (gated-chain bug, under retry-ergonomics), c-mo56pq2k (option-choice decision, under failure-and-retry-policy), c-mo56pspi (Slice A — extend SpiderStuckCause enum and write stuckCause at every failEngine call site; observability-only at MVP, no autoUnstick widening), c-mo56puvw (Slice B — retry primitive: attempt counter as rigs.length, stuck→open operator/clockwork command, retry automation bounded by cause). Linked Slice B depends-on Slice A and depends-on option choice; linked gated-chain-bug related option-choice (Options 2/3 fix it by construction). Two Coco-correction moments worth flagging: (a) initial stuck-detection framing assumed nothing existed — jumped to design before grounding in implementation, only caught when Sean instructed the code scan; (b) initial MVP framing treated serial-hopper milestone (c-mo28k3ar) as the whole autonomous-operation MVP until Sean probed with c-mo3d6lyd (decisional machinery), forcing a reframe to two orthogonal axes — execution mechanics and decision quality — with Patron Anima (w-mo4bke5x, in flight) covering the decision axis for MVP. Pattern to watch: jumping to design before exhaustive grounding in existing code."
    commissionable: false
    justification: sanctum

content.text:
Pushed. Here's the summary for the record:

---

# Session Summary

## What we did

- Oriented on the autonomous-operation click (c-mo1mq8ry) and picked up the stuck-rig detection line under the serial-hopper milestone.
- Exhaustive scan through the nexus code to ground stuck-detection framing: `animator/src/startup.ts` (reconciler + 90s staleness), `animator/src/animator.ts` (30s heartbeat), `claude-code/src/babysitter.ts` (DLQ + heartbeat), `spider/src/spider.ts` (failEngine, tryCollect, autoUnstick, trySpawn 1:1 check), `spider/src/types.ts` (SpiderStuckCause enum), `clerk/src/types.ts` (writ phase state machine).
- Reframed MVP: session-level timeout/reaping is already solved by the animator heartbeat reconciler. Real gap is structured stuck-cause taxonomy + retry primitive.
- Verified the gated-chain bug: `autoUnstick` walks original `blockerIds` and does NOT follow retries links — reposted writs strand dependents once `spider.follows` ships.
